```bash
pip install -U pook
```

If I build the Lua 5.2 from source code it won't be registered into pkg-config thus the setup.py of lupa won't be able to found it.

A, probably bad, walkaround is hardcode the path for my Lua stuffs such as:

````
def find_lua_build(no_luajit=False):

    return dict(
              extra_objects=["/root/lua/lib/liblua.a"],
              include_dirs=["/root/lua/include"]
           )
````

Then I could run `python setup.py install` to make it build and install.

BTW. Before install lupa I need to modify the Lua's make file to add `-fPIC` to CFLAGS.

Just to add my use-case for hooks.   I do a lot of ``pip install --user`` installs, and so, by default, I have the ``--user`` ``\bin`` directory on my `$PATH`.

When I activate a virtual environment, it can quickly get confusing if I am accidentally picking up a ``--user`` install by running a script that I forgot was in the ``--user`` ``\bin`` directory (Cython and ipython re the most likely culprits).  So, my `.bash_profile` works out where the bin directory is and stores it in `$PY_USER_BIN`, and I have this `post_activate` hook  (applied for all virtualenvs)

```
# Clear user Python binary path
export PATH=$(echo $PATH | sed "s|${PY_USER_BIN}:\{0,1\}||")
```

I mention it only because it seems to me that it might be a fairly common use-case.

Hey @skoczen I can't re-open this issue, so I thought I'd leave some analysis here. In short, your mixing of gevent and multiprocessing is going to keep biting you in the rear. I attempted to monkeypatch with (socket=False), but then the bottle gevent server hangs on a socket.recv() as it can never know that data comes back.

The quickest solution is to use cherrypy for the bottle server instead of gevent. (Any other wsgi container would work, e.g. gunicorn. If I had the time, I'd probably make a PR to drop bottle for Flask.) 

Here's a [quick diff](https://gist.github.com/wohali/4097f09002ad36798be3) that fixed things, as I don't feel like forking tonight. Also, using something other than gevent will let you reload bottle, which could be nice for dynamic reconfiguration without fully restarting will. :wink: 

My guess is that, with this, you can possibly back out some of your multiprocessing DNS work as well, though multiprocessing.Queue is typically a good option anyway.

References with some additional detail: [1](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/8678307/gevent-monkeypatching-breaking-multiprocessing), [2](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/16517796/bottle-gevent-and-threading-gevent-is-only-usable-from-a-single-thread), [3](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/13318608/streaming-connection-using-python-bottle-multiprocessing-and-gevent)

The problem here is that I rely on Python's own unittest module to the actual test discovering (part of the [Traditional](https://github.com/CleanCut/green#features) feature).  So it is the built-in module that is ignoring it.  Pytest wrote their own discovery code, so they can do what they want.

To change behavior, I would either have to monkey-patch every version of python that I support (not pretty), or write my own discovery code (ouch).  I'm not really interested in doing either when Python itself doesn't consider the case.

Of course, you or someone else wanted to do the work and submit a patch, I would be happy to review it and accept it (as long as it's not crazy code that's going to be difficult to maintain).

Hi,

Thanks for immediate feedback, - 
I am looking into it to day, and yes, the only dlib-object, the iostream socket is deleted before we call exit. (verified by printouts).

During the socket/close/delete, a timer is created (and global-clock thread started), 
and then removed upon successful socket close.

Inspecting the process by debugger at this time, before python exit, shows 4 threads, where we recognize the s.wait_or_timeout() thread, the global-clock thread waiting for more work to do.
There are no timers active (tm. size()==0).

If we then invoke function to terminate the thread (shutdown=true ,signal etc.), it terminates the thread, and everything works as expected during static desctructors on unloading the dll.

If we let the global clock thread just run, call the python exit, the next things that happens is that the dll get unloaded. At this time c++ invokes the static desctructors around in the dll,  in some order.

Attaching the debugger at this time reveals that *all* threads are gone, except the python main-thread which is doing the dll-unloading. 
Nevertheless, the waiters variable of the global timer clock is 1, and thus a WaitForSingleObject(....) is invoked during s.signal() (expecting the global timer thread to ping back).
 This call will never return, since the global timer-thread somehow escaped the scene without ticking down the signaler.waiters counter.

I am experimenting with adding a global timer thread stop when no more timers are left in the timer-list, i.e. *symmetric* solution to the 
timer_global_clock::add() that do start the thread if it's not running.
thus, the 
timer_global_clock::remove(..) could/should stop the thread, if there is no more timers active, keeping moving parts at a minimum.

Early tests indicates that this also do work, but I feel that's still a workaround, since I have not found the exact point where the timer_global_clock thread 'leaves the building' with no traces.
 -  except leaving the state of the timer_global_clock in disorder, that is, the state of the object indicates there is someone waiting &running, but there is no more threads running.

I continue work a bit on this to see if we can reveal the point where the global clock thread exits, I feel that could be useful if there is something that needs special care during shutdown.

Hey Anthony- so if I understand correctly, you want the intersection curve between the two meshes, rather than the watertight result mesh. I think the easiest way to do that is probably to 'color' the faces that exist on one of the two meshes, and then take the boundary of that coloring:
```
b = trimesh.primitives.Box(extents=[1,1,1])
c = trimesh.primitives.Cylinder(radius=.25, height=10)

# run the intersection using SCAD, it is generally more reliable than blender
i = b.intersection(c, engine='scad')

# the distance from the result triangle center to the original mesh
distance = b.nearest.on_surface(i.triangles_center)[1]

# if it's basically zero the face on the intersection result belongs to the box
on_surface = np.abs(distance) < 1e-5

# get a Path3D object for the outline of the boundary
boundary = i.outline(face_ids=np.nonzero(on_surface)[0])

```

Clients are stateless.  And I think these changes to placebo are more consistent with that since the decision to record or not is made based on the value of global state in the placebo module itself, not on any action in the client.  So, basically, if the global variable for recording_path in placebo is not None at the time the client is created, requests will be recorded.  So the starting and stopping of the recording is no longer done with methods in ``meta.placebo``.

I studied your code, and made a more or less a stripped-down version of what you had.

Basically, my script can send commands to Huawei S2300 via Telnet with some minor features:
1. It handles detecting invalid login attempt
2. It figures out the network prompt at run-time, and does support the case when the prompt changes due to mode change (such as being changed to configuration mode)
3. It does not use delay in order to determine whether the network device has completely returned output. Instead, it looks for the expected prompt. Ah right, this is actually expected behavior and is related to #110.

`bedtools genomecov` in default mode does not return a BED file. It's a general tab-delimited file representing histograms.

Demo time. Turns out we need the `{"5":True}` argument along with the specially-created `x` to get this issue to pop up in a minimal example.

```python
import pybedtools
x = pybedtools.BedTool("""
    chr1    1   100 feature1    0   +
    chr1    1   100 feature1    0   +
    """, from_string=True)
g = pybedtools.chromsizes_to_file({'chr1': (0, 200)})
y = x.genome_coverage(g=g, **{'5': True})
```
As you showed, the following raises `MalformedBedLineError`. `pybedtools` sees the first line and thinks it's a BED file, but then chokes on the second line:

```python
for line in y:
    print (line)
# MalformedBedLineError: Start is greater than stop
```
The solution is to bypass the interpretation of BED format.  For a file-based `BedTool` like this example, open the file indicated by `y.fn` and iterate over it. Or better yet, use pandas:
```python
import pandas
t = pandas.read_table(y.fn, names=['chrom', 'depth', 'bases', 'size', 'frac'])
#     chrom  depth  bases  size   frac
# 0    chr1      0    199   200  0.995
# 1    chr1      2      1   200  0.005
# 2  genome      0    199   200  0.995
# 3  genome      2      1   200  0.005
```

There you can see the problem on line 1. When it was trying to iterate over results, `pybedtools` thought "depth" was start position and "bases" was the stop, and sure enough depth is greater than bases.  

For streaming `genome_coverage` results, you'll need to iterate over `.fn`, which contains the generator of lines:

```python
y = x.genome_coverage(g=g, stream=True, **{'5': True})
for line in y.fn:
    print (line)
```

Note that everything works fine if you use one of the `genomecov` arguments that returns BED-like output, like `bg=True` which gets you bedGraph output:

```python
for line in x.genome_coverage(g=g, bg=True, stream=True, **{'5': True}):
    print (line)
```

Of course, the question is should `pybedtools` know about non-BED-like output and supply an appropriate warning or iterate over the file rather than convert to `Interval` objects. There's an issue #113 for that, but honestly it hasn't been a high priority and I'm not sure it warrants the additional complexity. Suggestions welcome!

Just for the sake of discussion: in my experience I expect the function sqr to return the squared norm instead of the product of an element by itself. A library of vectors, matrices, complex numbers or quaternions for sure needs to implement such a function (the square of the norm). I would call it sqr because the sqr function on real numbers also is equal to the square of the norm, so we obtain a homogeneous interpretation and nice polymorphism. In some situations the same algorithm on different fields can be written in the exact same way if we intepret the sqr function like that.

Of course one could expect sqr(q) to be q*q if we also have a group operation (which is not the case, for example, for 3D vectors). But usually when I'm computing q*q I'm working in a context where also q*q*q and q*q*q*q is needed, so I would not expect to have a function for the particular case n=2. If I use the function sqr(q) to compute q*q and I get a real number instead of a quaternion I would almost certainly get an exception and immediately understand what is happening. Instead if I use norm(q) and obtain the squared norm while expecting the norm, it is much more difficult to find the error.

Anyway: anyone is free to add its layer of user defined functions, so this discussion is not much relevant. If you just add a warning in the documentation I will be contented. Maybe a simple example such as:

```
>>> k = quaternion.quaternion(0,0,0,1)
>>> (2*k).norm()
4.0
```

Using only one IPDB instance also should work. The only question is how often you plan to instantiate IPDB: its start requires costs in terms of RTNL requests, but its runtime costs in terms of parsing broadcast RTNL messages.

Thus, IPDB create & release will be cheaper when it will be done rarely, and in case of frequent requests to IPDB will be cheaper to instantiate it once and then keep running instance.

Offtopic: pls keep in mind also that you always can use the low-level synchronous IPRoute, if you don't want complicated IPDB machinery. IPRoute doesn't require any significant time to start up, doesn't listen for broadcast messages by default, and just provides plain direct netlink API to the kernel.

(ps: I've also now seen this issue when using `.debug()` on a certificate file originating in the same place as this OctetString )

(See http://luca.ntop.org/Teaching/Appunti/asn1.html, Section 3 for info on the header values discussed below).

The first byte is `\x24`, which is `b00100100`. The 6th bit being `1` indicates it is constructed, with the remainder of the byte representing `\x04`, the tag for OctetString.

The second byte is `\x80`, which indicates the length is indefinite, and the string should be scanned, stopping at the first `\x00\x00` that is not part of a child record.

Thus, the value of this OctetString is everything after `\x80` and before the trailing `\x00\x00`. Thus, asn1crypto is returning the correct result for `binascii.hexlify(x.native)`.

The `openssl asn1parse` command is *further* parsing the contents of the OctetString. To accomplish this with asn1crypto, you'd need to use `core.ParsableOctetString` instead of `core.OctetString`. You can then try accessing the `.parsed` attribute to see if the contents are a valid DER/BER encoded value.

However, the contents of the outer constructed OctetString are not just a single ASN.1 value, but rather a concatenation of number of values together. I don't know that I have ever seen this in a spec, except when someone is trying to create backwards compatibility with an existing ASN.1 spec, but they want to add optional data to the end. The only instance of know of this is the `TRUSTED CERTIFICATE` encoding that OpenSSL uses to append trust information after a PEM-encoded X.509 certificate.

To handle this situation, you kind of need to know it is going to exist. I wonder if there is part of the PKCS#12 spec I missed that uses this, or if it is some legacy variation of the encoding? It is almost identical to the issue in #24. There a CMS structure had an indefinite length segment that was a concatenation of a number of smaller chunks of data. These can't be DER values since they use indefinite length, but it would be nice to know if the file is an anomaly, or something asn1crypto needs to handle.

The code below should be a proper handling of this construct using asn1crypto 0.21.1 or newer:

```python
# coding: utf-8
from __future__ import unicode_literals, division, absolute_import, print_function

from asn1crypto import core, parser
import binascii

data = binascii.unhexlify(b'2480040d8dfff0980736af936e423acfcc04159277f7f0e479ffc7dc33b2d03d7b1a186d4472aa490000')
payload = core.OctetString.load(data).native
print(len(payload))

values = []
while len(payload) > 0:
    info = parser.parse(payload)
    sub_len = len(info[3]) + len(info[4]) + len(info[5])
    values.append(core.load(payload[:sub_len]))
    print(values[-1])
    payload = payload[sub_len:]

```

Is what a conditional relationship?

In a `ManyToOne` relationship, because there are *many*, the *many* have the id reference to the *one*. That is what you want. Your `.parent`, `.left`, and `.right` are just pointers, and you don't need to have a reverse `OneToMany`. Just use a `ManyToOne` any time you need a pointer to something.

In the case of a tree, cascading deletes would be convenient if you could delete the root and have the entire tree delete itself, which is why I'll probably end up building a `OneToOne`, which will probably borrow much of the underlying code of `ManyToOne`, plus add cascading deletes. Who knows, this may even push me to build `ManyToMany` while I'm at it.

Yes, when `--hosts` is submitted, `testinfra_hosts` is just ignored, and I not sure we want to change this behavior.

When using ansible backend without specifing `--hosts` or `testinfra_hosts`, host are expanded from the inventory (or `all` if nothing provided), that's why it work for you. Keep using ansible backend will workaround this issue.

Since docker backend has no `inventory` notion, hosts list must be specified. You could ensure to have a `testinfra_hosts` in all your tests files for instance.

Related issue #48 to implement easily `skipIf` functionality based on current testinfra host list.

Also note that testinfra has [an API to handle ansible inventories](https://github.com/philpep/testinfra/blob/master/testinfra/utils/ansible_runner.py) that you could use in your tests using the docker backend (as long as you have access to an ansible inventory where inventory name is the related docker host name):

```python
from testinfra.utils.ansible_runner import AnsibleRunner

testinfra_hosts = AnsibleRunner("/path/to/inventory").get_hosts("mygroup")
```

I assume by "errors", you mean the estimates of the uncertainty, `varxi`.  It's just a straightforward [propagation of Poisson errors in the counts](https://github.com/rmjarvis/TreeCorr/blob/releases/3.3/treecorr/nncorrelation.py#L380).

The code doesn't currently make any attempt to estimate sample variance, so these are always underestimates of the true uncertainty.  To include sample variance, you should generally do some kind of [bootstrap](https://github.com/rmjarvis/TreeCorr/issues/50) or [jackknife](https://github.com/rmjarvis/TreeCorr/issues/24) style covariance estimate, but these are not integrated into TreeCorr yet, so you'd have to do them yourself.  (Or many people use theory covariances.)

Yes, the code should use MediaInfo_Open for windows, dos, os2, ce.  For darwin (mac) and linux, they should use MediaInfoA_Open
-- as per the actual MediaInfoDLL source.

This has to do with the source, which is C++, and receives the argument String for Open method.
As such they both are unicode based.  The difference in which method to use is based on which OS you are in, as the windows OS represent the data differently than others.
Given your adamant insistence on the matter of Unicode, It is a good bet you are on a Windows system which implements Unicode only in wchar, even though char is unicode compatible. And thus the reason for the two methods in the API.

I have taken the time to look up the source code, and refresh my memory of C++ (which i hate with a passion) to put this matter to rest.  When running on any DOS, OS2 or Windows variant, you must use wchar as argument to enable unicode, as these systems ignore unicode on char.  When using every other operating system, use char, because this is a String which is of type char, and unicode is always in play.

Hi,
I think the problem is not the output.

> Have you considered either implementing it inside the vcr_cassette fixture?

That would be a better solution in terms of the API, but it would require that we return something that behaves like a cassette, e.g. something like the `DummyCassette` I have implemented [here](https://github.com/sentinelsat/sentinelsat/blob/0ce3858dc73f041974ec23c3574225bc73c2e4b6/tests/shared.py#L63). An even better solution would be to return a `DummyVCR` as well, so if the user does 
```python
def test_something(vcr):
    with vcr.use_cassette('test_something'):
        ...
```
it will behave consistently with the rest of the cassette uses.

By the way, regarding that specific pattern (which I had to use in fixtures): the current implementation does not set the `cassette_library_dir` parameter when initializing `VCR`, so the cassettes created in that manner will land in the current working directory rather than the correct location. I suggest setting the cassette directory via that constructor parameter and ditching the `vcr_cassette_path` fixture. You can override the `cassette_name` fixture to get equivalent behavior.
For example, in `test_overriding_cassette_path` the relevant code becomes
```python
@pytest.fixture
def vcr_cassette_name(request, vcr_cassette_name):
    # Put all cassettes in vhs/{module}/{test}.yaml
    return os.path.join('vhs', request.module.__name__, vcr_cassette_name)
```

Hi, yes, this is now finally possible using the `ground_truth()` function in the `Pipeline` class (as of Augmentor v0.2.0).

Basically, just use this function to point to a directory containing the mask images, and when you generate new image data, images will be generated for both the mask and the original images:

```python
p = Augmentor.Pipeline("/home/user/images")
p.ground_truth("/home/user/mask_images")
```

Call `p.sample()` in the same way as per usual to generate both sets of images.

See the README for more details.

This speaks to the wider issue of pyodbc detecting the environment it is being used in - OS, OS byte-width, driver, RDBMS, etc. - and changing internal settings automatically (unless explicitly set by the client of course).  It seems this should be possible for the most part.

Not really.  There isn't a defined ODBC set of data for this, but it is pretty easy to get any of it:

First, you know it was successful or an error would have been raised.

The easiest is the number of rows processed: `Cursor.rowcount`.   This is defined in the DB API specification and is discussed on the Cursor wiki page.  If the query is an insert, update, or delete statement it will be the number of rows affected.  Otherwise, like the create table here, it will be None.

If your application is always going to want the time, there are multiple ways of doing it, but one that is handy for more than that is "wrapping" the objects.  Write your own Connection wrapper that defers everything to the base class.  Have it wrap any cursors.  Then in the overridden cursor `execute`, take the time before and after calling the base class one.  This is also a good way to add debug logging, logging of long running queries, etc.

I agree it would be a good idea to have this accessible from pyODBC; and ODBC already defines the constants needed:

- `SQL_TXN_READ_UNCOMMITTED`
- `SQL_TXN_READ_COMMITTED`
- `SQL_TXN_REPEATABLE_READ`
- `SQL_TXN_SERIALIZABLE`

However, given that this is only one of many other options accessible via connection attributes, perhaps it would be sufficient to add the constants and use `set_attr()` for this instead of adding a dedicated field, e.g.:

```
conn.set_attr(pyodbc.SQL_ATTR_TXN_ISOLATION, pyodbc.SQL_TXN_SERIALIZABLE);
```

I think it makes sense to add such a "convenience method/field" when it's part of DB-API or otherwise likely to be commonly used, but otherwise setting the isolation level is already available via `set_attr()`. Many ODBC drivers with extended functionality, like the SQL Server Native Client/ODBC Driver for SQL Server, expose that functionality through custom connection and/or statement attributes. @mkleehammer what are your thoughts on adding specific methods/fields vs. using the more generic connection/statement attribute interfaces?

Thanks for your patience @cuyu. I've just come back and had a look at this.

I can reproduce the same results as you're showing in your table, which means my previous assessment was incorrect - bash is adhering to its documentation (in that `${#COMP_LINE}` agrees with the two values of `COMP_POINT` in your table), and zsh misses the mark in both cases. I also don't think zsh is counting unicode characters, since the length of COMP_LINE in bytes in those cases should be 26 and 27 respectively. This looks like a bug in bashcompinit.

The good news is this means the code to adjust COMP_POINT is actually not necessary at all - I have removed it and written some extra test cases, and everything looks fine. The incorrect values of COMP_LINE in zsh will only impact completion if you try to complete from somewhere other than the end of the line, which is not so bad. The original test case you reported was likely only failing due to argcomplete being unable to write to the debug stream, even though it would never have been displayed.

So it could break a lot of things for the end user. Imagine a user has some sort of logic to reformat phone numbers to his specific needs. Like: `reformat_phone(value)`. How is it possible with the current implementation? Or any other functions/classes/etc which wraps values.

What I suggest?

## Lazy objects (or generator)

In my opinion, we should create `LazyField` wrapper to wrap any other existing field. And a special `fields` container with all the existing fields wrapped into `LazyField`. So, how would it work?

>>> from custom.utils import format_phone
>>> from mimesis.schema import Schema, fields
>>> schema = Schema('en')

>>> schema.load(schema={
...     "id": fields.cryptographic.uuid(version=4),
...     "name":  fields.personal.full_name(gender='female'),
...     "version": fields.development.version(semantic=True),
...     "phone": format_phone(fields.personal.phone),
... }).create(iterations=2)

On each iteration lazy objects (or generator) generates new value. User has all the control, code is more pythonic. 

## Considerations

> it sounds like conda is being used as a package manager like homebrew or apt-get for certain c\c++ libraries which is strange.

It can be, but there are good reasons for it. This is an example from another field: recently I have been working with GIS Python libraries, and many of them wrap some GDAL functionality. When one does `pip install gdal`, it actually downloads the Python wrappers and _compiles_ them against `libgdal-dev`, which is managed against the operative system.

As you can see here, the `libgdal-dev` version is tied to the operative system version:

https://packages.ubuntu.com/search?keywords=libgdal-dev

This means that if I want to use GDAL 2.x in latest Ubuntu LTS, I have four options:

1. Just don't do it, and use Docker or virtual machines to use a different operative system (like Arch Linux).
2. Look for some PPA.
3. Compile it by hand and put it in a different location so it doesn't break the system.
4. `conda install libgdal` https://anaconda.org/anaconda/libgdal/files

And this use case applies to tons of scientific Python packages.

> Also I don't understand the need for making a separate SPICE package

It is not mandatory, but would help in case someone else want to wrap SPICE in a different way, or use it directly from C, or whatever.

>  I have very few anaconda users downloading spiceypy as is (I have numbers that show an order of magnitude more pip installs).

There might be a number of reasons for this, and it might be difficult to understand them. However, for the record I would like to say that `conda install astropy` from conda-forge is getting more than twice the number of downloads than from the official channel.

https://anaconda.org/search?q=astropy&sort=ndownloads&sort_order=1&reverse=true

> at the moment I don't really see why this is worth doing

You don't have to, if you do not feel it's useful. I might do it myself in the future, though :) And of course I will ping you if I ever do.

I'm going to throw out a few ideas but I'm not a fan of any of them.

1. Add `@rule(remove_from=orgs, ...)`
2. `self.orgs.remove(...)` - but I guess this prevents the machine's way to reason about where data enters and leaves the system?
3. When we return a value for `target=` we actually return a wrapper with a `.remove()` and a`.get_value()` method.

No 3 could fix a related problem I often have: Depending on the state of my model I don't always have a valid target to return. I could return `NotValid()` to signal hypothesis to not store the returned value.

But no 3 is also much uglier and makes everything much less nice (implementation and interface).

Thanks for reporting this.

To clarify: There's not strictly speaking a conflict, because they will both be writing out the same thing. It's essentially content addressable storage - the file name is a hash of the source that's going to be written.

The problem is that Hypothesis can see that the file already exists and try to read it while the other process is still writing to it and instead reads an empty file. This is obviously silly and shouldn't happen I agree.

There are a number of ways this could be dealt with. I think my preferred option is to write to a temp file and then move it to the right location once it's written (note that this needs to be atomic, so we probably don't want to use /tmp/ because it's often going to be mounted on a different file system)

1. `require` is not really a problem because if you can use Babel to compile everything to the single file. Just like you compile ES6 to ES5.
2. Again not a big problem because you can simply define and use the pyimport **function** instead of the statement.
3. All the variables are stored in the variable called `var`. You can retrieve any object from it using `var.to_python().name`. 
4. Yes, I agree this is a problem, especially with confusing types like `unicode` vs `str` and `tuple` vs `list`. Also, keyword arguments are not supported in JS. No easy fix for this.
5. This could be a bug, I will check it out.

Yeah, compiling NPM modules may be sometimes a bit complicated because some of them depend on `node` host environment. For example on object like `process` or `Buffer` which is not implemented by Js2Py. It is possible to make everything work, but that would involve much more work on the Js2Py project. And I dont think its worth it just for a single interesting project.

Hi Matthew,

That doesn't seem right. On of the design goals of XlsxWriter is fidelity with the file format produces by Excel. In a majority of cases a file produced by XlsxWriter will be 100% identical to and indistinguishable from a file produced by Excel.

Perhaps the best thing to do would be to post a sample program the demonstrates the issue that you are seeing.

Opening the file in Excel and immediately saving it results in a file size change as Excel creates an additional sharedStrings.xml (amongst other changes) and the file contents will now be found in a Windows search.

That sounds like you are using the constant_memory mode which as a memory saving optimisation doesn't use the sharedStrings data structure.

Try turning that off and seeing if it fixes the issue that you are seeing.

Regards,

John

> I don't have a machine available at the moment to test but to solve your issue
> I would suggest to do something like this:
> ```
> server = iperf.Server()
> server.port = 1234
> 
> try:
>    server.run()
> except NotSureWhatExceptionGetsRaisedByDefaultForPortInuse:
>   server.port += 1
>   server.run()
> ```

The problem with this is that server.run() does not raise exceptions, so the ```except``` statement does not get called no matter what. Instead, the call to ```server.run()``` returns good JSON for a successful test or the JSON ```"error": "unable to start listener for connections: "``` in the event that the specified port was occupied. So one way to solve the problem would be:

```
server = iperf.Server()
server.port = 1234
server.run()
# wait about 2 seconds (that is the time it takes for server.run() to return with an
# error message in the event of an error)
# if server.run() has not yet returned assume everything went fine
# subsequent code here
```

But this is a hack. What if the amount of time it takes for ```server.run()``` to return with an error changes? I know that it is a big ask, but I think that a small change in code function is needed. There needs to be some way to tell, without any doubt, whether the server was able to start successfully. I can think of two ways of doing this:

1. Introduce state to the ```Server``` object. The ```Server``` object would have, for example, a boolean ```started``` property that was initially equal to ```False```. A call to ```server.run()``` would attempt to start the server, and if there were no errors the call would change ```server.started``` to ```True```. If an error occurred with starting the server a suitable exception could be raised.

2. Have ```server.run()``` return another object that represents the running server, rather than having a ``state`` variable. IMO this is worse because it is a bigger change.

In both cases we would need a new way to get data off the object from tests. A generator would be nice here (for getting data as it comes in during a test). Even a plain old property could hold the final results. Again, I realize this is a big ask with breaking changes so if it doesn't sound good I can just fork it and work on that.

I fixed the problem. For running correctly on Windows was needed to add two variables in env (subprocess.Popen ()).
```
self.env = {'PYTHONPATH': project_root,
            'ASK_VERIFY_REQUESTS': 'false',
            'SYSTEMROOT': os.getenv('SYSTEMROOT'),
            'PATH': os.getenv('PATH')}
```

In the travis test results, you dont see any test being run because I purposefully built the smallest possible test, providing only a virtualenv, nose and nose-exclude. Simply running this through setup.py nosetests (without specifying any exclude-dir) resulted in a crash.

I did more research and found new stuff:
- Removing "from __future__ import unicode_literals" fixes the issue (but I guess you want unicode?)
- Wrapping all add_option first parameters in str() fixes the issue (I'll push a PR for this)
- The issue lies within fancy_getopts: I found this bug from 2013 https://bugs.python.org/issue18936 and fancy_getopts code which is spot on:
            # Type- and value-check the option names
            if not isinstance(long, str) or len(long) < 2:
                raise DistutilsGetoptError(("invalid long option '%s': "
                       "must be a string of length >= 2") % long)

In this case the 64 bytes limit applies to your input/ouput/feature reports.

Feel free to post the 33 bytes HID report descriptor, on Windows only
drivers have access to this descriptor.

Usually, unless you'd be really familiar with HID report descriptors (
http://www.usb.org/developers/hidpage/HID1_11.pdf) you'd use macros
provided by your firmware SDK. Reason is HID reports schema is binary with
bit field level fields.

The rough way to design a HID report is to use the USB SIG HID report tool,
it's a GUI, but honestly is far from easy to use, this would only help you
for simple reports.

I remember I saw some script based tools on the web, you'd describe our
reports in XML/Json form and you'd get a header from it, but if you are
into firmware, I'd rather recommend getting C macros once you'd feel
familiar with the reports.

Other important item is validating your binary descriptor, also I remember
seeing some Linux parsers before, a quick search on google gave me this:
http://eleccelerator.com/usbdescreqparser/

Hello Hassan,

the only significant change in the behavior of apply_bpe is the 
treatment of UTF-8 whitespace and newlines. Before, they were treated as 
word boundaries, and silently converted into plain whitespace and 
newlines, which caused some bugs (especially the newlines part). The new 
behavior is to only treat plain whitespace as a word boundary, and 
special UTF-8 whitespace characters get treated as part of a word.

If you learn a new vocabulary with learn_bpe.py , there is also a new 
handling of the special end-of-line token to prevent the production of 
some spurious segmentations at test time: 
https://github.com/rsennrich/subword-nmt/issues/19

please let me know if this explains the difference you observe. If not, 
it would be great if you could share a minimal reproducible example of 
the different behavior.

best wishes,
Rico

3. The API is down or overloaded. To check whether that's the case check out the status page for Shodan:

The strategies are the responsible for defining the needed methods to access the current framework session capabilities (https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/b78159cbd45ff1c281e92d7756ab03c22f7ded8f/social_core/strategy.py#L182-L192), if that's what you are looking for, then you need to define you custom strategy (you can inherit from the already implemented one for the framework you use), and define those three methods with your desired mechanism.

And the source of the problem is in session_set function: it receives value as a list of unicode string: 

value = [u'foo'] 

and then sets the cookie as str(value), which is "[u'foo']".

When session_get is invoked, it reads the cookie as a string "[u'foo']", while expecting to receive a list.

If a significant users of this tool are from the US then it makes sense to make the legal implications explicit.

For example, one app sends data using this encoding method:
`00000071 05 75720000025B42ACF317F8060854E00200007870 00000058 1F8B0800000000000000636060486560CE49CC636660606062602BCE2F2D4A4E05711818D8CB528B8A33F341525A271958738BD33D53A0324076486501585D2A035B69716A1150EA3F10303046FD07009E25AC6854000000`. 
For now I know that `00000071` is 113, the length of next bytes to the end of message. I don't rly get, is this part of java protocol or not, but next bytes is header: `05` is seems to be version, not prefixed with magic number, next `75720000025B42ACF317F8060854E00200007870` seems to be header for `byte[]` object, next `00000058` is 88, the length of that array of bytes. And leftover `1F8B0800000000000000636060486560CE49CC636660606062602BCE2F2D4A4E05711818D8CB528B8A33F341525A271958738BD33D53A0324076486501585D2A035B69716A1150EA3F10303046FD07009E25AC6854000000` is contents (gzipped data because start with `1F8B` but this is another story). It seems it doesn't differs much from original java encoding because contents is not real class. But app that I work with is only sending `byte[]` like this. And for now I use this trick (read length, read and skip header, read length again, read contents) to decode and encode data.

The problem comes from the use of [``bytearray``](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#func-bytearray) in JavaByteArray, as it only accepts integers between 0 and 256, whereas Java bytes are signed, between -127 and 128.

All-day events are meant to be all-day no matter what timezone you're in, but EWS doesn't special-case this situation and just uses the standard datetime fields, which contain timezone information. When possible, we request datetime values in UTC since timezones in EWS are an absolute mess (see e.g. https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/dn789029(v=exchg.150).aspx).

Attaching existing items in Exchange to other items in Exchange (e.g. attaching a `Message` to a `CalendarItem`) is actually quite straight-forward. See https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib#attachments

If you can create a meeting invitation via exchangelib, then just use the `attach()` method and call it with the original `Message` object. This will attach the message when you call `save()` on the invitation. Beware, though, that meeting invitations are untested and quite poorly supported, so there's a possibility that creating a meeting invitation does not work at all. See https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/issues/89

To answer the immediate question, then yes, this code doesn't crash:

```python
from exchangelib.folders import Folder
Folder(folder_id='foo')
```

But to do anything useful with it, you would need to add an account and a changekey:

```python
Folder(acount=a, folder_id='foo', changekey='bar')
```

The `homematic.reconnect` service ([documentation](https://home-assistant.io/components/homematic/#services)) is what should be used to reconnect to the CCU. An automation can be used to trigger the reconnect.
Your solution solves the problem only partly. There can be multiple proxies, and only the one attached to the jsonRpc-stuff would know about the problem, leaving the other proxies broken. Hence it would be better to monitor a devices which reliably publishes data, and if no data has been received for a certain amount of time, call the reconnect service for the proxy for which updates stopped arriving at Home Assistant.

Of course if there's another reliable way to do this I'm open for that. But compatibility between different platforms (CCU, Homegear, RaspberryMatic etc.) is the highest priority for me.

The initial design was to have locks "id" as local state. IOW: "you lock it, you release it - it's your responsibility".

If, say, there are users who need to lock a resource during a course of multiple processes then they should manually carry over the state of the lock (the "id"). They can easily do that by creating `Lock("name", id="12kjh34gjh312g4kj2h3g")` in the right places and manually carry around the "id".

Hi Kevin,

Yes, I can confirm that this problem exists. There's no Python-Redmine fault in it though. ``ValidationError`` you're getting is coming from the Redmine itself, because for some reason the ``text`` attribute is always required (I should fix this in Python-Redmine docs for update operation). So unless this will be fixed on the Redmine side there's nothing we can really do about it.

The workaround I can recommend is to always sent the text attribute:

```python
redmine.wiki_page.create(project_id='test-project', title='ParentTitle', text='ParentText')
child = redmine.wiki_page.create(project_id='test-project', title='ChildTitle', text='ChildText')
redmine.wiki_page.update('ChildTitle', project_id='test-project', parent_title='ParentTitle', child.text)
```

I believe you can create all the wikipages first and put the created objects in a dictionary preserving the parent-child hierarchy and then iterate over this dictionary doing updates to the children assigning them to their respective parent pages and sending the same ``text`` attribute again.

Hope that helps.

> More info: prior call for getBusNumber() and getDeviceAddress() returned normally so the device was actually found.

Actually, if the device wasn't present at all, you shouldn't get a USBDevice instance. getManufacturer, getProduct and getSerialNumber are special as they need to open the device temporarily. I think the other libusb functions just read data from kernel's copy of device's descriptor, making them guaranteed to succeed. But as you demonstrated, opening is not the issue (...or at least no issue is detected at open level).

You can emulate libusb_get_string_descriptor_ascii call with (quick & dirty):
```python
import usb1, libusb1
print usb1.USBContext().openByVendorIDAndProductID(
  VID,
  PID,
  skip_on_access_error=True,
).controlRead(
  libusb1.LIBUSB_TYPE_STANDARD,
  libusb1.LIBUSB_REQUEST_GET_DESCRIPTOR,
  (libusb1.LIBUSB_DT_STRING << 8) | 1, # 1 being iManufacturer
  0, # Default language
  255, # How many bytes to read
)[2:].decode('utf16') # Skip 2 bytes: descriptor length and type.
```
If this works, try calling getManufacturer instead (replacing `openByVendorIDAndProductID` with `getByVendorIDAndProductID`), and gradually getting closer to the original code.

> Maybe the problem is with libusb1

At least, python-libusb1 will not generate such error on its own, so something wrong happens somewhere else. But its ultimate cause can be some libusb misuse on my (python-libusb1) part.

I can think of two more lower-level debugging methods:
- [usbmon](https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/usb/usbmon.txt) or via wireshark to get the full dump of exchanges with the device. This would show if the device is misbehaving (ex, a GET_DESCRIPTOR urb going down with no response/stall/...). [USB in a nutshell](http://www.beyondlogic.org/usbnutshell/usb6.shtml#StandardDeviceRequests) part on standard device requests can help understanding what is going on. If you sniff when plugging the device, you should see working GET_DESCRIPTOR requests for these string descriptor, which should help compare.
- executing python under strace, so as to find failing syscall if a libusb bug is suspected (ie, if usbmon yields nothing surprising)

I feel like I'm missing something, sorry for not providing more specific guidance. As a last resort, writing a quick & dirty C equivalent should be relatively easy, and then you can report the issue (...if C code also exhibits it) on [libusb's mailing list](http://www.libusb.org/wiki/MailingList).

Please provide code with a full example of what's breaking, what you expect to happen, and the full stacktrace of any exceptions you experience. There's not much info here, but this looks like a circular import issue. In short, the following lines

```python
from insec import user_datastore, security  # in main.py
```
```python
from main import app, db  # in insec.py
```
conflict with each other (one needs to be imported fully before the other can import it). See [overholt](https://github.com/mattupstate/overholt) (specifically, the [factory](https://github.com/mattupstate/overholt/blob/e03209f7a059d165a9154355d090738af3159028/overholt/factory.py)) for a good example of an application using Flask-Security broken out into several modules and packages.

\* Note: because this is more of a general Python issue and not a bug or feature request for Flask-Security, this is better suited for StackOverflow than the issue tracker.

This is a fun solution! I hate to shoot it down, but I strongly believe we should not monkey patch `Flask-Login` to inject graceful backwards compatibility into it; neither should we make any attempt to support multiple versions of `Flask-Login` in this library.

If we *were* going to go with this solution, `is_authenticated_compat` would belong in the `Flask-Login` project and not this one.  I think you're going to get a lot of push back on that from @maxcountryman (and for good reason).

## I recommend we solve backward compatibility issues with dependencies

The `master` branch needs this in `requirements.txt` ASAP as a hotfix / minor release:

`Flask-Login>=0.1.3,<0.3`

The `develop` branch needs this in `requirements.txt`, and the develop branch needs to treat `is_authenticated` as a boolean instead of a callable:

`Flask-Login>=0.3`

The next time there is a major release, flask-security simply drops support for earlier versions of `Flask-Login` through plain and simple dependency management.

Let's not over engineer this! This is precisely what dependency management exists for: managing backward compatibility. **There is simply no reason that future versions of `Flask-Security` need to support past versions of `Flask-Login`**

At a glance I do not believe this extension to be vulnerable to that type of attack for two reasons.

First, this extension doesn't currently support public/private key encryption for the JWTs (pull requests welcome for this!). So it only ever uses a secret key for signing the keys, which shouldn't be leaked like it was in that article.

Second, anytime we encode or decode a JWT, we use the algorithm defined in the extension [here](https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/blob/master/flask_jwt_extended/config.py#L142), which [default to HS256](https://github.com/vimalloc/flask-jwt-extended/blob/master/flask_jwt_extended/jwt_manager.py#L143). We never use the value that is defined in the JWT itself. That said, I do need to check the underlying PyJWT implementation and make sure it's doing what I expect it to be doing. Regardless of pagination type, you will see in the parameter description the values of the default limits and the maximum limit of the each page size, example para **LimitOffsetGraphqlPagination**:

Have you checked [crontab](http://docs.celeryproject.org/en/latest/reference/celery.schedules.html#celery.schedules.crontab)? Let's say you have a periodic task `foo` that should be ran at 12pm for every client. You could do something along these lines:

```python
# Run the task every hour.
@periodic_task(crontab(minute='0'))
def run_foo():
    for tenant in get_all_tenants():
        # Make sure connection's tenant is set correctly.
        with connection.set_tenant(tenant):
            run_time = TaskRunTimes.objects.get(task_name='foo')  # Get run time for the tenant.
            schedule = crontab(minute='0', hour='12', nowfun=lambda: datetime.now(pytz.timezone(tenant.timezone))
            if not schedule.is_due(run_time.last_run_at):
                continue

            run_time.last_run_at = datetime.now()
            run_time.save()
            foo.delay()
```

Basically, what it does is that for each tenant it stores the last run time of the task in the database. Combined with tenant's timezone, you can check if the task should be ran or not. The downside is that it always runs the check for all the tenants.

Alternatively, you can iterate over a list of timezones, and do the check earlier so that it does not loop over tenants that should not be run.

```python
# Run the task every hour.
@periodic_task(crontab(minute='0'))
def run_foo():
    for timezone_name in get_tenants_timezones():
        run_time = TaskRunTimes.objects.get(task_name='foo', timezone=timezone_name)  # Get run time for the timezone.
        schedule = crontab(minute='0', hour='12', nowfun=lambda: datetime.now(pytz.timezone(timezone_name))
        if not schedule.is_due(run_time.last_run_at):
            continue

        run_time.last_run_at = datetime.now()
        run_time.save()
        for tenant in get_tenants(timezone=timezone_name):
            # Make sure connection's tenant is set correctly.
            with connection.set_tenant(tenant):
                foo.delay()
```

The benefit of the second solution is that you don't iterate over all the tenants to check if the task should be ran, but only for tenants from timezones you are sure it should run.

If you're using a version of Django that old, you need to use django-admin-sortable v1.4.9 or below. The latest version is not compatible with Django 1.4.x

I created a new project using Django 1.8 and SQLite3. I removed the Cryllic portions of the code for simplicity and set up an app called "books" with the following models:

    from django.db import models
    from adminsortable.models import Sortable, SortableForeignKey


    class Record(Sortable):

        class Meta(Sortable.Meta):
            pass

        author = models.CharField(max_length=255, default='Someone')

        def __unicode__(self):
            return self.author


    class Page(Sortable):

        class Meta(Sortable.Meta):
            pass

        record = SortableForeignKey(Record, related_name='pages', null=True, blank=True, default=None)
        updated = models.DateTimeField(auto_now=True, null=True)

        def __unicode__(self):
            return '{} ID: {}'.format(self.record.__unicode__(), self.pk)

Yes, that might be the issue.  There is a slight race condition at the end (for InputMode.SPARK), where the data feeding job is done (and therefore Spark starts the shutdown process), but the chief worker is still exporting the model.  A simple workaround is to add a sleep statement just before the TFCluster.shutdown() method in your driver.

1. `{'dev1':mx.cpu(), 'dev2':mx.gpu(0)}`

The root cause seems to be the incompatible MKLML-library defined in the MKLML-Dockerfiles:
```
RUN wget --no-check-certificate -O /tmp/mklml.tgz https://github.com/01org/mkl-dnn/releases/download/v0.10/mklml_lnx_2018.0.20170908.tgz
RUN tar -zxvf /tmp/mklml.tgz && cp -rf mklml_*/* /usr/local/ && rm -rf mklml_*
```
This MKLML-version only seems to be compatible to Ubuntu 14.04. 

Updating the library with the following causes the test to pass - make sure to update MKLML to v0.11 if you're running ubuntu 16.04:
```
RUN wget --no-check-certificate -O /tmp/mklml.tgz https://github.com/01org/mkl-dnn/releases/download/v0.11/mklml_lnx_2018.0.1.20171007.tgz
RUN tar -zxvf /tmp/mklml.tgz && cp -rf mklml_*/* /usr/local/ && rm -rf mklml_*
```

The problem is that currently is output grad is not the immediate output of `_backward_CachedOp`, the storage types of `_backward_CachedOp` outputs are inferred as dense storage. This is because _backward_CachedOp didn't register `FInferStorage`, producing dense outputs by default. 

In this example, the outputs of _backward_CachedOp are passed to `add_n` to produce row_sparse grad. 

To solve this issue, we need to register `FInferStorage` for `_backward_CachedOp`, which performs subgraph storage type inference, and return the stype inference result to the caller.

Here is an simple example:
######################

from mxnet import nd, gpu, autograd, gluon
import numpy as np
import librosa

ctx = gpu(2)
fft_length = 256
fft_size = fft_length // 2 + 1

f = gluon.nn.Dense(1, flatten=False)
f.collect_params().initialize(ctx=ctx)

wavs = nd.array(librosa.load('210001.wav', sr=16000, mono=True)[0], ctx=ctx)
x = nd.random.uniform(shape=(1, wavs.shape[0], 1), ctx=ctx)

third_dim = x.shape[1] // fft_length + 1
padding_needed = wavs.shape[0] % fft_length

wavs = nd.expand_dims(nd.expand_dims(wavs, axis=0), axis=0)

f(x)

print f.weight.grad()

with autograd.record():
    y = f(x)
    y = nd.swapaxes(y, dim1=1, dim2=2)
    y = nd.concat(y, nd.zeros(shape=(1, 1, padding_needed), ctx=ctx), dim=2).reshape((1, 1, -1, fft_length))
    wavs = nd.concat(wavs, nd.zeros(shape=(1, 1, padding_needed), ctx=ctx), dim=2).reshape((1, 1, -1, fft_length))
    stft_y = nd.contrib.fft(y, compute_size=fft_length).reshape((1, 1, third_dim, -1, 2))
    stft_wavs = nd.contrib.fft(y, compute_size=fft_length).reshape((1, 1, third_dim, -1, 2))
    stft_y = nd.sqrt(nd.sum(nd.square(stft_y), axis=4) + 1e-12)[:, :, :, : fft_size]
    stft_wavs = nd.sqrt(nd.sum(nd.square(stft_wavs), axis=4) + 1e-12)[:, :, :, : fft_size]
    loss = 0 * nd.mean(nd.square(stft_y - stft_wavs), axis=(1, 2, 3))

loss.backward()
print f.weight.grad()
####################

Using this custom build with win32_rdcw_buf_size set to 4096 definitely changes things for the better!

Overall wall time for applying a Mercurial stream clone bundle is nearly identical to no watchman.

When I profile things, gone are the ~1ms calls to `ReadDirectoryChangesW()`. Replacing them (as reported in procmon) are a bunch of `NotifyChangeDirectory()` calls. Times on these are varied between 20-300us. There are a lot of them. But overall CPU utilization of watchman is low - maybe 1% - and stack sampling doesn't reveal anything alarming.

Since the overall CPU usage of watchman was lowered, I was initially worried that latency is being introduced elsewhere. But I could find no sign of that. So I think your theory about passing a too-large buffer into the kernel which results in the kernel silently rejecting it and allocating a new one is very plausible!

Playing around with different values, `65536` appears to be 20-30s slower than `4096`. (This could be within variance, however.) Similar procmon behavior in terms of reported function calls. Ceiling for `NotifyChangeDirectory()` looks to be a bit higher though - 500-600us. I only spot checked things though. It would be great to see a distribution curve of these timings to confirm my suspicions. That would make sense: bigger buffer takes longer to fill and more time before you get a notification and more time for watchman to process events in a filled buffer.

`131072` appears to be similar to `65536` in terms of Mercurial operation time. However, procmon didn't report any activity in watchman.exe during the Mercurial operation. watchman.exe was still consuming ~1% CPU. Stack sampling showed `WaitForMultipleObjects()` and `ReadDirectoryChangesW()` being in the stack the most, with the former being ~2x more common. And the stack under `ReadDirectoryChangesW()` is completely different with this patch applied. No more heap related symbols.

 `262144` is ~30s slower than `131072`. Same procmon behavior as `131072`.

`16384` seems to have the same wall time behavior as `4096`.

There are some ongoing changes to asyncio support in master, so if you are using a released version of pyzmq try looking at the examples from [the same version](https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/v16.0.2/examples/asyncio/coroutines.py).

The main change: pyzmq < 17 requires an [initial step](https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/v16.0.2/examples/asyncio/tornado_asyncio.py#L12) to tell asyncio to use `zmq_poll` as the underlying event loop. pyzmq 17 removes that requirement and thus removes the `zmq.asyncio.install()` call from the examples.

So I made a mistake: as mentioned in the anaconda thread, pyzmq's conda package *does* depend on zeromq, and it links against it correctly. But it doesn't link against libsodium. So the problem with pyzmq not having fast crypto in conda is probably a problem with zeromq's packaging there, not pyzmq.

The remaining non-ideal situation for pyzmq is that its bundled zeromq does not include libsodium, so a pip-installed pyzmq has slow crypto. Even if you have libsodium and libzmq, pip install by default on linux installs a precompiled wheel which seemingly doesn't use libsodium.

On second thought, I think this is a reasonable idea, but I'm not sure there's any reason it needs to be distributed with monkeytype; it can be a separate project (`pytest-monkeytype` that depends on MonkeyType. Some further thoughts on the idea:

1. `monkeytype run pytest` should already offer similar functionality, but having it as a pytest plugin would certainly be more convenient, and enable more integration with e.g. pytest config. For a parallel case, you can `coverage run pytest`, but there are also pytest coverage plugins.
2. A plugin that enables monkeytype on every test run would only be useful to projects that don't measure test coverage, since monkeytype and coverage can't coexist. But it would be possible to make the plugin smarter so it can easily be turned on or off with a flag.

The original proposal *was* backward compatible (`async` was treated as a keyword in only a few contexts); but with Python 3.7 `async` is being promoted to fully-fledged feature of the platform. As noted in that PEP documentation:

```
async and await names will be softly deprecated in CPython 3.5 and 3.6. In 3.7 we will transform them to proper keywords. Making async and await proper keywords before 3.7 might make it harder for people to port their code to Python 3.
```

Unfortunately, this is going to break several modules though, including `pyhive` and `omniduct`.

I'm torn in the case of `omniduct` as to whether I want to the relevant variables with `async_`, or change to `sync` or `wait` and invert the logic. Sincec I usually encourage users to pass in these values as keyword arguments, appending the underscore is a bit tacky in my use case.

The root cause is `django-elasticsearch-dsl==0.5.0` requires `elasticsearch<7.0.0,>=6.0.0`
while `elasticsearch-dsl==5.4.0` requires `elasticsearch<6.0.0,>=5.0.0` and then conflicts happen.

Currently Pipenv's resolver can't compromise with older package version to match the requirement constraints. You need to manually specify older version of `django-elasticsearch-dsl` in your `Pipfile` so that it depends on `elasticsearch<6.0.0`

@wichert That might make sense too (I'm actually not totally clear on in what circumstances you'd want to have both a setup.py and a Pipfile), but if you have a line in your Pipfile that says:

```
Django = "*"
```

you're telling pipenv that you want it to install _any_ version of Django. If what you really want it to do is install 2.0 or lower, tell it that instead:

```
Django = "<=2.0.0"
```

While in this particular case pipenv is upgrading Django for no real reason, it could be that somewhere down the line you try to install a package that requires Django >2.0.0, at which point pipenv will happily install it if you haven't told it that you need <=2.0.0.

Hmm, you're right there is indeed a `[3J` in linux as well:

```
       The action of a CSI sequence is determined by its final character.

       J   ED        Erase display (default: from cursor to end of display).
                     ESC [ 1 J: erase from start to cursor.
                     ESC [ 2 J: erase whole display.
                     ESC [ 3 J: erase whole display including scroll-back
                                buffer (since Linux 3.0).
```

Actually, it looks like uploadsrv doesn't care about the content-type at all - `MultipartDataGenerator` just passes `application/octet-stream` for all data. I wasn't paying enough attention before when I assumed that this was a significant value. Presumably uploadsrv ignores this value anyway, and gets the image type from the header of the actual image data (which makes sense).

If that's the case, then rack parsing the request into a string (ie: no filename/content-type/ doesn't actually drop any information that uploadsrv cares about, so it shouldn't be hard to get uploadsrv to handle the string case. Unless I'm misreading things, that seems like the cleanest solution.

It's a bit more work than just passing dummy file names, but it does make the interface exposed by the actual HTTP API cleaner (and anecdotally, people do seem to like writing their own uploadsrv clients, so I'm +1 for this).

Yes. That's the reason. 

In general, the json file should store the actual parameters of the job. A proper way of coding this example would be to do a defensive copy of whatever params is passed to the ExampleJob, e.g., `self.params = dict(params)`, a separate variable is used to hold the current total, and the ExampleHandler reads the current value from the job from a file written by the job rather than passing a common dict between them. That way, the json will always reflect the actual value being passed in. 

So this example violates a few good coding practices. i. mutable default arguments are a bad idea. ii. having two separate classes modify the same param variable. I only wrote the example this way to avoid having to write to file and read back from file for a simple example.

Hi @astro313 
This is the kind of question that would be great for the [scikit-image tag](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/scikit-image) on StackOverflow! You're also likely get a lot more eyes on your problem there.

The syntax to call blob_dog is the same, regardless of the number of dimensions your input grayscale image has. The input array doesn't need to be 2D but it does need to be grayscale, otherwise you might get results that weren't what you'd intended/expected.

It's not clear what kind of input image data you have. I'm guessing most likely 2D RGB images, since you ask about [`rgb2gray`](http://scikit-image.org/docs/0.14.x/api/skimage.color.html#skimage.color.rgb2gray)? Images like that would appear as a numpy array of shape `(y_dimension, x_dimension, 3)`, where the three planes are the red, green and blue channel intensities, respectively. The data you've loaded in the example above `binary_blobs` is binary (boolean type numpy array), not color or grayscale - so it doesn't make a ton of sense to apply the [`rgb2gray` function](http://scikit-image.org/docs/0.14.x/api/skimage.color.html#skimage.color.rgb2gray) on it. 

Assuming that is the case, here's an example with a 2D color image as input:
```python
from skimage import io, data
from skimage.color import rgb2gray
from skimage.feature import blob_dog

astronaut = data.astronaut()
gray_astronaut = rgb2gray(astronaut)
blobs = blob_dog(gray_astronaut)
print(blobs)
```

@liewegas  I disagree I see state in:

The local config file.
The nodes config files.
The nodes installed packages.
The command line used.
The cluster, 
The services running.
The operating systems running.
The pools that exist.
If the server is up.

Having this in one data structure, (sqlalchamy or simple python dicts) would allow us to make the code that establishes these state gathering functions to exist once rather than be duplicated.

We can then derive what to do:

If the operation should succeed.
One way to query all these different components state.
One way to compare desired and current state.

We can also use the state gathering code, to validate the state transition, and also inject state to make unit tests easily.

Personally I think MVC adoption makes the code a lot simper, in terms of branching and duplication, as views can be made to focus on only one thing, where as most ceph-deploy functions are mix many features and include conditionals, of if in this state or on this OS, where as they could be split into functions that are much smaller, that either do some thing (Views) or decide what to do (controller).

MVC patterns can be nested, and can share models. I dont care about the implementation.

Just my opinion :)

It appears that this RFC didn't reach consensus, so no decision was made. Let me try to rekindle the discussion with some organization and some proposals.

The discussion started from code style, then touched code quality, the use of frameworks, testing, and documentation. I think these are very different problems, which require different discussions.

1. JavaScript code style: IMHO, not as important as the others, but still nice to have. Let me mention [JSCS](https://github.com/jscs-dev/node-jscs), a configurable tool to enforce code style conventions. Once we decide on a style guide (or perhaps we can lean on the defaults of some project we like), it should make the job much easier.
2. JavaScript code quality: I think there are two parts to this. First we have the style of JavaScript files: a tool that was already mentioned is [JSHint](https://github.com/jshint/jshint), which will require a discussion on the settings. Then there's the problem of the obtrusiveness of the JavaScript in A LOT of the templates. Judging from the output of `ag onclick` this will require A LOT of work.
3. Use of JavaScript frameworks: consensus in #2110 is that this shouldn't be pursued.
4. Testing JavaScript libraries: RFC #2221 is still open, but, from what I've seen, Jasmine is the de-facto standard.
5. Documenting JavaScript: RFC #2845 is still open, and this is where it should be discussed.

Related to these, and in particular to the first two, is the question of having some kind of task runner for JavaScript tasks, like Grunt or Gulp. Should I open an RFC for this?

In summary I propose to:

1. Use this RFC to discuss JSCS's settings
2. Start a new RFC about JSHint's settings
3. Start an effort to refactor the templates
4. Use the existing RFCs to discuss testing libraries and documentation solutions
5. Start a new RFC about a JavaScript task runner

`transaction >= 1.5` seems to break these tests. See #19.

The `keep_session` functionality was added specifically to support testing, letting you to inspect the session after the transaction has finished. See: https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.sqlalchemy/blob/master/src/zope/sqlalchemy/README.txt#L181

The `tpc_abort()` method on the two-phase data manager is called when a two-phase commit is aborted after `tpc_prepare()` is called (i.e. another data manager aborted during the prepare phase.) Not calling rollback here would leave the transaction wedged. In Postgres calling prepare disassociates the transaction from the postgres session:

> From the point of view of the issuing session, PREPARE TRANSACTION is not unlike a ROLLBACK command: after executing it, there is no active current transaction, and the effects of the prepared transaction are no longer visible. (The effects will become visible again if the transaction is committed.)

http://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/sql-prepare-transaction.html

So I don't think there'd be anything left to inspect in the sqlalchemy session following the `tpc_abort()`.

We can agree we're focused on 2a - Resultset and not-indexed value.

I think this is coming from the resultset containing objects that do not have an entry in the boolean index.

AFAIK - and I could be wrong, when catalog.reindexObject(obj) is called, and it does not have a field corresponding to the boolean index, the index is never touched.    Please confirm this.

The test case looks like this:

        obj = Dummy(1, True)
        index._index_object(obj.id, obj, attr='truth')
        obj = Dummy(2, False)
        index._index_object(obj.id, obj, attr='truth')
        obj = Dummy(3, True)
        index._index_object(obj.id, obj, attr='truth')
        # obj = Dummy(4, None)  ##Don't call index._apply_index
        # obj = Dummy(5, None)  ##Don't call index._apply_index

       res, idx = index._apply_index({'truth': False},
                                      resultset=IISet([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))
        self.assertEqual(list(res), [2])
        ##Fails - list(res) is [2,4,5]

So the question is, what should we do with these objects that aren't in self._unindex?

Path 1a says remove objects that are not in self._unindex  `intersection(index, resultset)`
Path 2a says keep objects that are not in self._unindex `difference(resultset, index)`

doing a difference removes only those objects that have an entry in the boolean index, leaving the other objects in the result, where as doing an intersection on the inversion will remove the objects that don't have an entry in self.unindex

And I argue that "remove them" is the correct answer for path 2a:
   `intersection(difference(self._unindex, index), resultset)`

But, for a pathquery, all content items have a path.
So the `resultset` comes in containing a bunch of objects that don't have entries in self._unindex.

Previously, dthkao (David T.H. Kao) wrote…
My concern is ambiguity between pre_setup and setup_class. In order to get args for some generated tests we'd need to move a lot of the logic from setup_class to pre_setup anyway. Is there any reason the try loops for setup_class can't just be pushed ahead of fetching the tests list? For a decorator for test functions, there are some nice points in usage. In stead of an explicit call generate tests, you could use the decorator to mark which test methods generate tests (as is done already), and if setup_class occurs before calling fetching test names, then in the reflection in _get_all_test_names we can have the logic to generate test_names (basically what's inside generate_tests at the moment). Usage could be @generated_test(name_function, arg_sets) def test_do_stuff(self): # test logic
Decorator does not work well for this. People may need to write some code to create the arg_sets, e.g. a list comprehension with string formatting. This code needs to be executed somewhere, before setup kicks off, to generate new tests. Decorator does not provide such a venue. The order of setup_class affects how the record is tallied, changing its order requires a lot of changes. setup_class is also the place users register controllers today, whereas all we want is to have a pre-process that decides what tests there are. This is actually a precursor to be able to list all the tests in a test class, which is required for CTS/GTS integration. If generate_tests is mixed into setup_class, it would be impossible to do list tests without also initializing all the controllers. Overall, I chose this approach because it is less invasive to the current test execution flow, and leaves room for future expansion. --- *[mobly/base_test.py, line 445 at r1](https://reviewable.io:443/reviews/google/mobly/197#-KjHR4v4KmKd2Z8NrFCk:-KjJO7tltyjaK-wHYrMa:bcuzqtu) ([raw file](https://github.com/google/mobly/blob/0a179964b1992b6583e8fc3fcce673e6511339b7/mobly/base_test.py#L445)):*

To me, the zipline build doesn't feel like quite the right medium for building pyfolio packages.  

- Right now the zipline jobs build conda packages for the versions of zipline that they test (currently 2.7 and 3.4).  I believe pyfolio has historically run ahead of zipline in its support of newer python/numpy/pandas, so probably we'd only build pyfolio packages for those configurations that zipline supports?
- The zipline jobs currently build conda packages only for zipline and zipline's dependencies, and this process adds a bunch of time to each job.  I'd prefer not to have zipline developers waiting while their branch also builds packages that zipline doesn't need.

What do you think about having the pyfolio job build pyfolio packages (and any missing packages for its dependencies, e.g. empyrical), in the same way that zipline does?

```xml
<system.webServer>
  <staticContent>

    <mimeMap fileExtension=".woff2" mimeType="font/woff2" />
  </staticContent>
</system.webServer>
```

I took a look at this. Sadly I haven't managed to fix the problem. However, I believe the problem resides in the [globalhotkeys C code](https://github.com/Guake/guake/tree/e0fde824f865462e875be5613d9b3a835051d274/src/guake/globalhotkeys).

In the file [keybinder.c](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/e0fde824f865462e875be5613d9b3a835051d274/src/guake/globalhotkeys/keybinder.c#L236) during the initialization it calls [`gdk_window_add_filter`](https://developer.gnome.org/gdk3/stable/gdk3-Windows.html#gdk-window-add-filter), where the first parameter is a window and the second parameter is a function that will be called for each event produced in the window. As it is being called now, it does it with the root window `gdk_get_default_root_window ()`. Reading the documentation for `gdk_window_add_filter` we find:

> Pass NULL for window to get all events for all windows, instead of events for a specific window.

This is what I tried, and it did not make any difference.

If we uncomment the 21st line in [keybinder.c](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/e0fde824f865462e875be5613d9b3a835051d274/src/guake/globalhotkeys/keybinder.c#L21) and recompile it by running `./dev.sh`, we will get additional terminal output when pressing F12. I have highlighted the more interesting sections in bold.

If we change workspace in Gnome Shell to an empty one, i.e. where we don't have any window in focus, and press F12, these sections aren't produced. Therefore we can draw the conclusion that the keypress isn't detected since the output on line 168 in [keybinder.c](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/e0fde824f865462e875be5613d9b3a835051d274/src/guake/globalhotkeys/keybinder.c#L168) is never produced.

The other thing I noticed is that since `httplib_test` monkey patches httplib during test run, it breaks other two tests: `requests_test` under py2 and `pynamodb_test` under py2/py3. This is because before adding `httplib` support, all the SDK patched libraries are independent.  But `httplib` is a dependency of `requests` and `pynamodb`.

The fix is to have some unpatch logic on httplib patcher so that upon `httplib_test` finish it reverts the module in the runtime to the original one so other tests are not impacted. I'd really appreciate if you have extra time to this fix.

The broken tests also show us how you can deliberately patch `httlib` (even you don't directly use it) to get more insights for some libraries depend on it. But also this would be dangerous as per https://github.com/aws/aws-xray-sdk-python/blob/master/aws_xray_sdk/ext/httplib/patch.py#L57 this line throws an error if `_prep_request` is not called. If an upstream library (in this case `requests`) use its own `prepare_request` method, this will break. It would be better if `xray_data = getattr(instance, _XRAY_PROP)` also handles key doesn't exist so that all three methods patched in `httplib` can be called independently.

These are some useful information I think I could share with you since you mentioned on patching `httplib` for X-Ray usage.

Now that I'm working with some examples that require some slightly more sophisticated field masks, I've found an issue with the utility.

Consider the following case where we want to update a campaign:
```
def main(client, customer_id, campaign_id):
    campaign_service = client.get_service('CampaignService')
 
    # Create campaign operation.
    campaign_operation = client.get_type('CampaignOperation')
    campaign = campaign_operation.update
    campaign.resource_name = campaign_service.campaign_path(
        customer_id, campaign_id)
    campaign.status = client.get_type('CampaignStatusEnum').PAUSED
    campaign.network_settings.target_search_network.value = False
    fm = protobuf_helpers.field_mask(None, campaign)
    campaign_operation.update_mask.CopyFrom(fm)
 
    # Update the campaign.
    campaign_response = campaign_service.mutate_campaigns(
        customer_id, [campaign_operation])
```

It produces the following CampaignOperation:

```
update {
  resource_name: "customers/9185018835/campaigns/1455799401"
  status: PAUSED
  network_settings {
    target_search_network {
    }
  }
}
update_mask {
  paths: "resource_name"
  paths: "status"
  paths: "network_settings"
}
```

Those are two distinct things. What you cited is the naming restriction for CloudFormation resources which is much stricter than the an SQS QueueName. The QueueName is specified here as a basestring:
https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/blob/master/troposphere/sqs.py#L29

There probably is an issue to improve the vaidation of that name. But what you're wanting is probably something like this (untested):
```
from troposphere.sqs import Queue

mydeadletterqueue = t.add_resource(
    Queue("CFQueueWithoutUnderscore", QueueName="SQS-Queue-With-Underscores"))
```

It looks like `Metadata` is an attribute for every resource (ref: [Adding Comments inside AWS CloudFormation Templates](http://blogs.aws.amazon.com/application-management/post/Tx2CE9T00GRYVHD/Adding-Comments-inside-AWS-CloudFormation-Templates) and [AWS Metadata Attribute doc](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-attribute-metadata.html)). *Metadata* is a valid key at the top of an CF template, too.

As you pointed out, every *Metadata* attribute can have a *Comment* key, but *Comment* is not a attribute for every resource. What is the best way to put *Comment* inside of *Metadata*?

When I search the troposphere code base I find *Metadata* in `__init__.py`, `autoscaling.py`, and `cloudformation.py`. For the latter two the `Metadata` class is based on the `AWSHelperFn` class. For `__init__.py`, *Metadata* is just listed as an attribute. Why is there this difference? My first stab at the answer is that while all resources have a *Metadata* attribute, each resource has different keys within that it expects. A map resource with a *Metadata* attribute will not be able to do anything with a "AWS::CloudFormation::Init" key, but a LaunchConfig resource will be able to use it.

Instead of `__init__.py` declaring Metadata as a simple attribute of every *BaseAWSObject*, maybe it should be a class that could be extended for each resource if it needs it. The *Comment* key can be a method of this class or just an attribute. I'm not sure which would be easier to validate or more intuitive to use. Does this accomplish our goal? (yes, I am asking before I actually tried coding it. :scream_cat:)
