The remaining AwsHelperFn objects that don't represent a real AWS Intrinsinc Function are very problematic and I haven't found a way to convert them in a backward-compatible manner.

I got really close with the "Tags" object provided in troposphere's __init__. It cannot be translated into an AWSProperty because it is a list of tags, making the props = {} impossible to write. I wrote a new AWSProperty called "Tag" with a proper 'props' object (Key/Value as required basestrings), and then changing Tags for a subclass of list that internally creates Tag objects and acts as a list of such. Then I changed a lot of the 'Tags: (list, False)' into 'Tags: ([Tag], False)' and got clean test runs, but this introduces a breaking change if anyone actually passed a list of {Key: k, Value:v} dictionaries because the inner types are now checked. Before, it only made sure it was a list.

The other major problem will be the Metadata elements from the cloudformation module. I never worked with them but I believe they don't have a set structure like typical properties. For those, maybe it should be better to just wrap them into a new no-validation object that would be separate from AWSHelperFn, instead of trying to wrap them into actual AWSProperty objects...?

one thing to mention here is that hash-based coverage dir naming is only used in the event of combined coverage for multiple test targets in the same run (i.e. `./pants test some/thing::` which globs multiple targets vs `./pants test some/thing:thing` which addresses a single target).

with a single target:
```
$ ./pants test tests/python/pants_test/pantsd:pailgun_server
11:09:10 00:00 [main]
...
11:09:12 00:02     [pytest]
11:09:12 00:02       [run]                     
11:09:12 00:02         [chroot]============== test session starts ===============
                     platform darwin -- Python 2.7.10 -- py-1.4.30 -- pytest-2.6.4
                     plugins: timeout
                     collected 5 items 
                     
                     tests/python/pants_test/pantsd/test_pailgun_server.py .....
                     
                     ============ 5 passed in 0.47 seconds ============
                     Name                                     Stmts   Miss Branch BrMiss  Cover
                     --------------------------------------------------------------------------
                     src/python/pants/pantsd/pailgun_server      61      6      8      4    86%
                     
11:09:14 00:04     [junit]
11:09:14 00:04     [go]
11:09:14 00:04     [node]
11:09:15 00:05   [complete]
               SUCCESS

$ ls -1d dist/coverage/*
dist/coverage/tests.python.pants_test.pantsd.pailgun_server
```
thus simply by changing the style in which you invoke tests - something roughly equivalent to a `for i in `./pants list some/glob::`; do ./pants test $i; done` - will produce coverage dirs named after the test modules.

if this doesn't work for you, then a `--test-pytest-coverage-outputdir` or similar option should be fine to add.

I have been thinking about doing "two-dimensional" parallelism for
some time for base backup volume uploading and think it is feasible to
scrape together a convincing PoC in ten days with moderately serious
work (based on little intuition, perhaps ten days for a true expert
working in a focused matter).  The area that PoC will probably fall
down is backwards compatibility, one shall see.

Currently, the tar segmentation process is based, as you correctly
wrote, on *whole* files being packed into tars to amortize some of the
amount of key bloating in S3 when dealing with lots of small files.
This works great for parallel and pipelined restore.  In this model,
if you imagine every parallel stream of execution as a series of
parallel lines on a grid (as follows), you get "one dimensional"
parallelism:

```
             |---About 1 GiB---|
part_0001.tar------------------>
part_0002.tar-------------->
[...]
```

This design has these meaningful defects:

It's not good at is making the cost of retrying chunks of work
smaller, and it materializes a large number of bytes in a temp
directory to assess the size of the upload, which must be known
up-front and is hard to guess a-priori with compressed streams.  This
is closely related to your problem with Lambda.

This amount of thrashing is not adaptive and tied to how much
parallelism one can get (the `-p` flag): on big machines and with
additional work to teach WAL-E SMP (the buffer management is now keen
enough to place the bottleneck in OpenSSL, so I need more processors
to get significant gains), one may reasonably want much higher
parallelism numbers, taking advantage of 10GigE.

Secondly, the materialized temporary files can get large enough that
the kernel may start evicting pages and thrashing, causing unnecessary
work.  I'd like that to ideally stop using temp files to simplify that
whole affair altogether.

Instead, I'd like to do this:

```
                  |--5-25MiB--|
part_0001.001.tar ------------|
part_0001.002.tar             \------->
part_0002.001.tar ------|
part_0002.002.tar       \----------->
[...]
```

Here I show how a second dimension, seen in that "." after what is
currently the volume number, can be used to tie together a stream.  I
think these volumes can be between 5MiB to 25MiB.

This will require more complex prefetching and uploading logics, but
seeing as how I've implemented such behaviors with success under more
complex constraints I think it's tractable.

What do you think?

On Mon, Nov 21, 2016 at 2:45 PM earsdown <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> @fdr <https://github.com/fdr> I think a better approach would be to
> continue to support both versions of python for the time being. Critical
> patches and bugfixes aren't getting backported to 0.9.x, which basically
> means 0.9.x appears to be dead. There doesn't seem to any good reason to
> drop support for python 2 this soon.
>
Well, maybe. It's not that simple. Maybe patches welcome, depending on how
invasive it is.

Consider some anecdote: years ago I had to backport  a Python 3.2-ish
version of subprocess.py to attain a fix for a file descriptor leak years
ago. I reported the bug to Python 2, and was told that the project was dead
and there would be no backport outside a security issue. Sometime later, I
was forced to drop Python 2.6 support rather suddenly because the Azure
library did so. If I did more research, I can probably come up with more
problems. All in all, I felt that considering the:

* Limited maintenance budget of WAL-E (i.e. my copious free time once in a
while and a smattering of bug reports and I only am invested in one
platform)
* Its stability: major changes haven't happened in quite some time, v0.9 is
fine and if there are backport requests I can take them
* Momentum of bit-rot issues

That I would try to cut the supported versions of Python, rather than more
than doubling them (by crossing the 2 vs 3 divide *and* handling 2.7 and
3.4 and 3.5).

So that was my reasoning. If we need to find a backporting method, we can.
But I assess that WAL-E change is so slow now-a-years that by the time 0.9
ages into obsolescence there will be fewer issues.

For what it's worth, I use WAL-E on Amazon Linux, which is kind-of CentOS6
like and, yes, I install a python3 and run pip-3.4 (now pip-3.5) and it
works well.

I must admit I liked that idea first. But think about it this way:

- The `--simple` option is actually a performance optimization. If you don't use it the worst thing that happens is that your test execution takes longer. Right? - If we make `SimpleTestCase` the default and you use `context.get_url()` (or the like) in your test code your tests will fail by default for using a behave-django or Django feature.

We run the `LiveServerTestCase` for convenience to make our job easier. Nothing should fail by default.

(If something is vertically-aligned, that doesn't mean a single-line row should have different top- and bottom-padding)

Confirmed that the replay system consults the JSON key Ipwb->Replay>Index when the IPFS is not running. 

To replicate:
1. Run `ipwb index 5mementos.warc | ipwb replay` with IPFS daemon running
2. Access webui, note memento.us URI-Ms
3. Kill ipwb, kill ipfs, re-run with `ipwb index froggie.warc | ipwb replay` _without_ relaunching IPFS daemon.
4. Note URI-Ms are still `memento.us`.

Since HTTPS is a strict requirement for IPWB's replay system to function properly on non-localhost domains, it might as well be a good idea to completely run it under HTTPS, even when served at localhost.

Something like this should do the trick (`pyopenssl` needs to be added to the `requirements.txt` file).

```python
context = 'adhoc'
# Supposing that cert and key are variables holding corresponding file paths, supplied at run time or from the config file
if cert in not None and key is not None:
    context = (cert, key)
app.run('0.0.0.0', port=PORT, ssl_context=context)
```

This approach will make sure that when cert/key paths are supplied, it will use those, otherwise fall back to an ad hoc cert/key pair (which the user will have to white list in the browser, same as self-signed certs).

This thinking has merit but to my mind there is a difference in a
traditional dep, like say charm-helpers and what layers provide. charm
build is a build time operation that takes care of things like boilerplate,
and wiring certain types of code (interfaces for example) into a runnable
whole. It is a more specialized tool than a package manager because it
attempts to be both content and process aware. By content I mean (again)
things like infterfaces where we can introduce special handling at build
time. By process aware we hoped that the whole charm tools suite could help
shepherd a new charm author to a successful working charm quickly, but also
help them keep that charm up to date within the larger ecosystem.

I admit there is a great deal of room for improvement here, perhaps some of
this is better done through a more sophisticated interfaces.juju.solutions
site which could track deps so as indicate impacted charms and layer at the
point at which a change to the index is being made. In truth though it is
more complex that even that, we often point to a repo which will change
through an index which wont so now it's a slightly more complex problem.
This process of keeping a charm up to date though has a content aware
signaling. The idea being not just that an interface has rev'd a version
but if there are any new states or data to respond to.

It feels like this has a home in charm-tools and not as much through
something like pip. Either way I hope people feel it is worth a session at
the sprint. Thanks for tracking this down and bringing up the issue.

My theory is:
- When the worksheet updated, React automatically re-runs the `render()` method for `Worksheet`, which produces some new data (the version gets updated?)
- So `WorksheetItemList`'s `render()` gets re-run, and it produces the exact same output as the previous render had produced.
- Since there's a React optimization for not re-rendering unless some props have changed (which can be altered in shouldComponentUpdate), the `WorksheetItemInterface` and `TableItemInterface` never re-render, since no data has changed.

So that last `render()` method to run is `WorksheetItemList`'s.

@balta2ar, first of all and most prominently, I agree with the concept that we should not get Coursera's servers being hit hard.

This was less of a problem with the old-style courses, since with just one request, we could grab all the syllabus/index file and process it locally with as long as we wanted with BeautifulSoup.

Now, with the new-style courses, things are much harder connection-wise, since we need a lot of requests just to get the videos (let alone all the other material).

Perhaps where users would see some improvement in performance would be to make parallel the download of materials (after everything is parsed and the URLs are already known). This would be akin to the move from HTTP/1.0 to HTTP/1.1 with persistent connections.

On the other hand, it makes implementation, debugging etc. harder. I guess that we can make this a low-priority, wishlist bug that, perhaps, we may implement some time in the future...

Otherwise, we have other, more pressing things to fix.

I want to note that NI-SCOPE and NI-FGEN APIs both have "binary" versions of their read/fetch and create/write functions. They exist mostly for performance and memory use and their value in the Python API is diminished if we have to copy each point one by one into a Python list. So basically, no point including them in anything other than numpy flavored methods.

> if I change sample rate to 2000Hz, t2-t1 is still 770ms.... it seems that setting sample rate doesn't take effect.

DAQmx hardware cannot support an infinite range of rates and the supported rates vary based on your hardware's capabilities.  DAQmx ends up coercing the supplied rate to a supported value.  

You can see this by calling:
```python
print(task.timing.samp_clk_rate)
```

Tip
- You can use [`AnalogSingleChannelReader`](https://nidaqmx-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/stream_readers.html#nidaqmx.stream_readers.AnalogSingleChannelReader) to read numpy data natively rather than `task.read(...)` which returns python lists.

> I'll stop bugging you

You're not bugging me.  If anything, I appreciate all the nice catches you made.  There's a lot of concurrency going on in here and I think I handled most of it pretty well, but I'm nothing but appreciative for being called out on the spots I missed.  :)

As to the rest of your question... it's a good one.  There _is_ a certain commonality between adapters and drains.  Aren't they are both just "handling" a message after all?  I actually thought about that pretty early on in the refactor, but deliberately decided against it.  Here's my justification:

To start, the interfaces for storage adapters and drains shouldn't actually be the same.  A component handling storage inherently _must_ be able to carry out a very important operation that a drain cannot-- _retrieving_ what's been stored.  So, I believe a storage adapter is its own distinct and specialized thing.

If not for the above we might conclude that storage adapters and drains are just different sorts of "handlers."  Then it would make a great deal of sense to manage a configurable _chain_ of handlers-- all of which can do _something_ with a log message before passing that message on to the next handler in line.  The first would store, the second would drain...

But if we accept my earlier justification for storage adapters and drains being two distinct things, then I ask myself why we'd ever need to concurrently use more than one _storage adapter_ or more than one _drain_.  What would it even mean, for instance, to use more than one storage adapter?  What would be the point of storing log messages on disk _and_ in memory.  When they're retrieved, which of those two would we retrieve them from?  It doesn't make too much sense.  I guess a stronger argument could be made for using multiple drains (even though that's probably an edge case), but configuring a _chain_ of drains would mean some significant and possibly breaking changes to how drains are configured in etcd.  Remember that even though my implementation is new, drains have been around in the old logger component for quite some time and we're obligated to keep the refactored logger backwards compatible with the old logger drain configuration.

With all of the above in mind, I was ultimately content with my conclusion that storage adapters and drains are two distinct sorts of things and multiple implementations of each are possible, but there's never a need to chain storage adapters and rarely, if ever, a need to chain drains-- and that's probably too much of an edge case to justify the complexity of making that possible while _also_ maintaining backwards compatibility with the old, single drain configuration option.

Does any of that make sense?  I'm not sure.  It's late.  :tired_face:

Installing ethash on Windows:
1. Clone the project from github
2.  Open \src\libethash\mmap_win32.c for editing
3. Add the following after the last #include statement
#pragma comment(lib, "Shell32.lib")
4. Open \src\python\core.c
5. Replace:
#include <alloca.h>
with:
#if defined(_WIN32) || defined(WIN32) 
	#include <malloc.h>
#else 
	#include <alloca.h> 
#endif
6. Run the following in the source code main folder
setup.py install

The reason that `buildTransaction` raises the error you see when `data` is provided is that `buildTransaction` is intended to be used to call contract functions with encoded function parameters.  This data gets encoded into the `data` field for the transaction and thus, if you supply `data` then you aren't actually calling the function in question.

If you want to override, then I suggest simply calling `buildTransaction` without the `data` field, and then modifying the returned transaction dictionary with the `data` you want.  However, at this point, you might as well just call `w3.eth.sendTransaction` if you want to do this.

Honestly, this is only the second case I can think of where there's mutating of a single file, rather than, e.g. using the '.d' directory best practice.  The only other bad actor I know of is Jupyter's extension configuration.  Given that it's so rare, I probably don't have as much patience for it as I should.  But... right now, I'd probably strongly resist any type of hard-coded special casing for this (or Jupyter, for that matter).

What I'd supported is hard-coding in conda-build that just wipes out the file, as @jjhelmus suggested.  If it's that important, whatever tool that is can use a design that's compatible with the technical needs of package managers.  Nobody does well with overlapping paths.  (At least we've evolved to the point where we're able to warn or block when we encounter them.)

Also, I can probably add tests, if you would like to accept this.

Conda used to report such 'clobbering' and took steps to prevent it, disallowing it to happen. The problem is it is impossible to prevent it when you have independent endeavours creating packages with overlapping files.

We *could* make a new 'gcc' metapackage and put it in the new 'main' channel so that it gets selected in preference to the old one (we do this for 'libgcc'). 'gcc' was never meant as a user-facing package anyway, it was always just a stop-gap compiler so we could compile things that needed C++11 so there was never *any* advertising around it.
Definitely a problem.  Thanks for the report.  I'll investigate.

What Conda does do seems a lot like an 80% solution that requires a fair bit of manual labor (to identify and list files needing relocation).  For binary files... C strings are assumed.  Is UTF-16 supported?

Maybe Conda can learn to do better over time?

And maybe we need a better (and more liberally-licensed) alternative to [the GNU `relocatable-prog` module](https://www.gnu.org/software/gnulib/manual/html_node/Supporting-Relocation.html), but getting open source projects to use it will take time.

> In general, regarding user package dirs for R and Python, maybe we should consider and env. var that controls whether this is used or not? Not sure whether we'd used the same one for both ecosystems or not?

That would be really useful I think. The main issue as I see it isn't so much corralling things like `install.packages` and `pip` to use conda, but rather to make it so that if someone *did* use that in the past, they aren't stuck with a seemingly broken installation of conda because the user-directories that `install.packages` and `pip` used have masked the conda packages in a binary-incompatible way. It's really a precedence issue honestly, if the user-packages were last in the import/library order, then this wouldn't really be an issue either.

That's basically what lands us in hot water, we distribute an `environment.yaml` file to our users, which is *amazing*, however if they use R or Python on their own (especially R) they end up with user package installs which mask our environment, and there's not a easy way to remedy that (for a long time, we had users write an `.Rprofile` file to modify `.libPaths()` as that was the best solution we knew for a while, and that was only after we realized our packages weren't the things that were broken). Requesting that they never use R/Python outside of conda is also not a realistic requirement for most.
Should be a duplicate of #3478.
For an immediate fix, you can always do `conda config --set sal_verify false` while we try to figure out where this is coming from.

Converting `"None"` to `None` is dangerous and error-prone.
Consider the case in which the value is expected to be a string.
Is `"None"` or `None` the intended value?
I could easily see doing things that way breaking an application that has the string `"None"` as a valid value in its config.

I'm not sure that the `None` should be written to the config file in the first place.
That seems like a bug or at least ripe territory for misuse.
I would expect null values in a config to simply be omitted when it's being written.

Are there use cases in which including a `None` value in your config is actually desirable or necessary?
I can't think of any, but maybe there are?

Recently I needed to parse the MD string directly from SAM, instead of going through a pileup file. While I haven't assembled a pull request yet, here, in brief, is one way of parsing the MD string and locating the positions of nucleotide mismatches:

```python
import re
import numpy as np

def RepresentsInt(i):
    '''
    Helper function that returns True if `i` is an int.
    '''
    try:
        int(i)
        return True
    except ValueError:
        return False

def parseMD(mdstr):
    '''
    Returns a numpy array with positions for where a `fromMM` base is, in the
    reference genome. This can then be used to intersect with the query string
    to find all desired mutations.
    '''
    # Split MD String at every single [ACGT] or ^:
    mdSub = re.sub(r'([\\^]*[ACGT]+)[0]*', ' \\1 ', mdstr)
    mdSplit = re.split('[ ]+', mdSub)

    mutArr = np.array([]).astype(str)

    # Iterate over Array and replace all mutations from the MD string with the
    # letter of the corresponding reference.
    # eg: 2G1 will produce the numpy array: 'M M G M'
    # All ^[ACGT]* by "D" and the number with a corresponding stretch of "M"
    for i in range(len(mdSplit)):
        mdPos = mdSplit[i]
        if len(mdPos) > 0 and RepresentsInt(mdPos):
            mutArr = np.concatenate((mutArr, np.repeat("M", int(mdPos))))
        elif re.match('\\^', mdPos):
            mutArr = np.concatenate((mutArr, np.repeat('D', len(mdPos) - 1)))
        elif len(mdPos) == 1:
            mutArr = np.concatenate((mutArr, np.array([mdPos])))
        else:
            # I'm not yet quite sure, if this won't just break at some point.
            # In my BAM/SAM files I have seen rare cases with two consecutive
            # mismatches in the MD tag causing this series of ifs to report incorrect
            # positions if I don't catch this.
            mutArr = np.concatenate((mutArr, np.array(list(mdPos))))

    # Return mismatch positions
    return mutArr

mdstring = read.get_tag('MD')

if not RepresentsInt(mdstring):
    mutLocation = parseMD(mdstring)
```

Using `RepresentsInt` allows me to only search the MD string if it contains mismatches (and therefore is not an int). The returned `np.ndarray` now gives you all positions of each mismatch (represented by the letter of each NT), match, or deletion.
You could expand the regex to capture [A-Z] instead to be fully SAM compliant, but keep in mind that you'll need to encode deletions and matches differently then.
Using a numpy array allows you to easily extract the position of specific mismatches, too.

The purpose of highlighting is to quickly spot all probIems in a (long) log and to do that we must address  all sources of problems. We should highlight compile time as well as run time problems, regardless of the simulator and the log/check solutions being used. I also think this is a very good feature which should be activated by default just like the coloring we have for pass/fail today.

This is best done by having a Python-based solution looking for specific words/phrases so I  don't think we should have special formatting that needs to be activated in the testbench. I did a very quick test and just looking for error/warning/failure/fatal followed by a non-alphanumeric character takes you a long way. It will do good detecting compile warnings and errors when using the simulators we support and it works well for VUnit check/logs, VHDL assert, and OSVVM/BVUL/UVVM solutions. It spots problems found by checks/asserts added to the testbench as well as problems detected by the simulator, for example division by zero. A few exceptions I found in this quick test
* When running VUnit under VHDL-93 the assert stopping the a successful simulation is highlighted. Adding some report statement to that assert like `"Exiting VUnit simulation"` would make it easy to spot that exception.
* Compile errors with GHDL results in `Failed to compile ...`. Whether such phrases should be included by default or be simulator specific is up for discussion

Regardless of the intelligence being built-in there will be false detections but I don't really see that as a major problem. Usually when you look at a log it is because you have a test that failed and if that log has two rather than one highlighted lines where one is a false detection you will still able to quickly spot the real issue. I rather have this situation than a solution which fails to highlight the problem line due to a too specific detection pattern 

For those that still want to improve on the default behavior there should be a way to configure the detection pattern. I don't have experience with very many tools supporting generic problem highlighting but the plugin for Jenkins I know of uses this approach so it seems to be a generally accepted solution.

Turns out the workload script sends an invalid `Cookie` header. It currently just gets the `Set-Cookie` header from the response, and re-uses it as the `Cookie` header.     In [1]: from django.http.cookie import parse_cookie

    In [2]: parse_cookie("csrftoken=********; expires=Mon, 05-Nov-2018 12:35:24 GMT; Max-Age=31449600; Path=/; sessionid=********; expires=Tue, 07-Nov-2017 12:35:24 GMT; HttpOnly; Max-Age=86400; Path=/")

    {'': 'HttpOnly',
     'Max-Age': '86400',
     'Path': '/',
     'csrftoken': '********',
     'expires': 'Tue, 07-Nov-2017 12:35:24 GMT',
     'sessionid': '********'}

`azure` and `azure-cli` are not compatible together, since they don't have the same purpose:
- `azure` is designed SDK and will install the latest packages of each service
- `azure-cli` is designed CLI/shell experience and SDK is an implementation detail, and having the latest of each SDK is not a requirement as long as each command is able to deliver the features needed.

TL;DR; developping SDK using `azure-cli` is a bad idea.

However, having CLI and a working SDK environment in the same shell makes total sense. What I would suggest is to use two virtualenvs, and link the `az` into your default PATH. So you can type `az` to get the CLI, and still develop using `azure`. Note that this is what CLI installers do: they don't install on your system site-packages, they install a venv. You could install the CLI on Windows with the installer or Ubuntu with the apt repo and get this installation for free.

Final note, `azure` itself is considered a "quickstart" package, and dev for production should be done by targetting individual package (i.e. `azure-mgmt-compute`).
Also, a change log entry should be added for the core module that says this bug has been fixed on Windows.

This sounds like a really cool idea -- and I think the latest updates which add `commit-msg` hooks could support this idea.

The concerns I have about this:
- What about `--no-verify`?
    - Neither `.git/hooks/pre-commit` nor `.git/hooks/commit-msg` run when `--no-verify` is passed, so nothing happens
- What about `SKIP`?
    - This could be detected by a cli and adjust the number of hooks
- What about hooks that don't affect any files?
    - I'd still consider these hooks to have "run", but trivially -- it'd be quite difficult to figure out which ones were applicable without replicating the entire logic of pre-commit in a hook
- How would a client hook know if all the hooks passed?
    - Simply put, it wouldn't but I don't think that's actually a problem -- in the case of `pre-commit` hooks failing, it would never get to the `commit-msg` hook which does the modification.
    - When another `commit-msg` hook fails, even if the hook has done the modification to the commit message the commit would still get properly cancelled

If you don't mind, I'm going to create another repository and start hacking away on this idea -- I think this is a good one :)

Excluding certain ascii characters (like space, newline and comma) makes it easier to design the tag editing UIs, because you can assume that those characters delimit tags. Opening up unicode brings in a whole range of lookalike characters that might cause confusion, but I'd be OK with adopting certain classes of unicode characters (e.g. the same set that are valid Python 3 identifiers).

I'm inclined to start with a pretty small set of characters, because it's much easier to allow extra characters later than to retrospectively disallow characters people may already have used. All of the use cases I've discussed with people so far can easily be handled with the limited set of ASCII characters I suggested.

We convert the magic commands to make it valid Python *syntax*. We don't get rid of them entirely, because some of them may affect what the code does. So if you've used magic commands, you have to go through and decide what to do with them: whether you can get rid of them entirely, or whether you need to replace them with more general Python code.

For some magic functions, we might be able to guess what you want, but not all of them, and the ones where we do guess we wouldn't always get right. So as `import this` says:

> In the face of ambiguity, refuse the temptation to guess.

Discussing the implementation will be easier with someone who you can talk to in person, but this is how I think about it:

*Events*:
- Consumer listens for events using a method called something like `on` or `register`.
- The same event may fire multiple times, and consumers are called each time.
- In the JS event system we use, events are identified by a name, not an object; this is convenient, because you don't need an event to be defined before you subscribe to it.

*Promises* (/futures):
- Consumer waits for completion using a method called something like `then()`.
- The promise is only completed *once*, but the consumer code can tell if it's already fired, so code that needs to run after it is complete can be run reliably.
- Promises are objects, so you need the promise to be defined before your code can use it.

Hey Richard. I think I delayed responding to this in part because I don't have any ideas for how to help, other than speeding up autograd in general.

I don't think elementwise_hess is doing much unnecessary work. It might be possible to exploit symmetry as you suggest but I'm skeptical that would provide much of a speedup in most cases because it would only save a factor of 2 inside operations that are already vectorized and it wouldn't reduce the Python or autograd overhead at all.

Depending on the dimensions involved, it's possible that forward mode differentiation would compute the Jacobian faster. As you know, computing the Jacobian of a function f: R^n -> R^m is generally faster in reverse mode when m > n, and faster in forward mode when n > m (though forward mode might have a 2x advantage since it only makes one pass). Do you know the typical shape of the Jacobian being calculated there?

However, I think the big win would be a compilation strategy like in #47. If so, it might be better to track any progress in that issue.

I ran into this issue while getting UTXOs for the registrar.

My solution was to modify pybitcoin (the registrar still uses it for RPC calls to bitcoind) to support 0 confirmation UTXOs and use a bitcoind node that watches the addresses I'm interested in.

This solution is not really applicable here but just pointing out that a reliable UTXO provider has been a major pain point for the registrar as well and a good solution to it can be very helpful.

We should also think about what happens when the UTXO provider goes down (individual calls to APIs like Blockcypher have failed for me either because of some temporary error or because of exceeding their rate limits).

The other issue to keep in mind is when broadcasting transactions different providers and API providers have different checks on which transactions will they accept. For certain corner cases (e.g., with low TX fees) I had to run bitcoind nodes that had very low dust fee limits etc and which could accept a wider range of transactions and relay them to the network.

You will get a similar output when using something like this:
```
from androguard.core.bytecodes.axml import AXMLPrinter
with open(filename, "rb") as fd:
    a = AXMLPrinter(fd.read())
    print(a.get_buff().decode("UTF-8"))

# try to load with an XML Parser:
from lxml import etree
# this one will fail:
etree.fromstring(a.get_buff())
```
(btw the androguard output is even more correct, as it acknowledges the namespace prefixes before the liapp crypted attributes)

The only problem is the empty namespace, which is added by Liapp:
```
xmlns:Liapp_Empty_00=""
```

This can not be parsed by an XML parser like xml.minidom or lxml.
Even if you set it to some value, you will have no chance parsing the file because the Manifest file is also crypted in such a way, that you can not read the entries:
```
 <application f3RNY="@style/Theme.AppCompat.Light" CRyfs="@string/app_name" BH2l8="@drawable/app_icon" nGvIZ="com.lockincomp.liapp.LiappCommon" 2SKNT="false">
```

I talked to a colleage and he said that the liapp packer will probably load the manifest, decrypt it and then run the main application.

In this case you would need to crack liapp first to decode the AXML file :/ From my current point of view, this is all I can do :(

- The signal handler raises an exception and complicates any soft error handling (retry / graceful shutdown).
- Blocks usage in multi-threaded applications. 

Seems like the "requests" library used internally by the f5 library natively supports timeouts.  May be interesting to strip out any code related to signals and rely on timeouts provided by the "requests" library when doing this API call at initialization.

It's not possible to retroactively add new subdomains to an existing certificate. Domains are part of the certificate and Let's Encrypt has to sign the entire certificate (otherwise anyone could just add *any* domain). You will have to request a new one.

Note that you can pass multiple `-d` arguments to the client, e.g.:
`-d example.com -d www.example.com -d example.net`

This would create a SAN certificate covering all three domains. SAN certificates increase the rate limit for each domain only by one, even if multiple subdomains are included. If you can somehow create your certificates in batches, this would be an option to work around the rate limits.

There's also a [rate limit override request form being worked on](https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/mass-registration-on-a-single-domain-from-a-router-vendor/7910/9?u=pfg), but no ETA on that yet.

seek() may only be called on partitions that are being actively fetched.
subscribe() is an asynchronous and it typically takes anything from a couple of milliseconds to session.timeout.ms to receive an assignment. So by the time you call seek() there will most likely not be an assignment yet.

Additionally, doing subscribe() and then seek() on a specific partition won't really work since you dont know which partitions will be assigned to you.

If you want to set the start offset for the partitions that are assigned to you you should provide an on_assign callback to subscribe(), it will be called from poll() when your consumer gets a new assignment.
In the on_assign callback you can set the starting offset of each partition by updating the provided TopicPartition list with an .offset for each partition.

Something like this:
```
    def my_on_assign(consumer, partitions):
        for p in partitions:
             # some starting offset, or use OFFSET_BEGINNING, et, al.
             # the default offset is STORED which means use committed offsets, and if
             # no committed offsets are available use auto.offset.reset config (default latest)
            p.offset = 1234
        # call assign() to start fetching the given partitions.
        consumer.assign(partitions)

     consumer.subscribe(mytopics, on_assign=my_on_assign)
```

> Why is the root name called figure? Shouldn't it be something like "page" or "root"?

I chose `figure` because I have something like matplotlib in mind : a figure in which you can put several maps/axes. I thought we could get closer to their function names. I would be okay for "page" or "root".

> Why is plugin a child of maps?

A plugin generally adds something on the map. But you're true, it can require to add somthing to the overall figure. This is why I have designed the thing so that each node knows who is his parent, and can have an effect on it. For example, if you have something like 

    Figure
        map1
            boatmarker1
            boatmarker2
        map2
            boatmarker3
            boatmarker4

Then each boatmarker will do something like

    boatmarker.map.figure.header['boatmarker_js'] = Link('http://...')

But as it is the same Figure object, they will simply overwrite the former header.
BTW, this is why we need to use `OrderedDict` instead of lists : order is important, but we also need to get objects by their key/name.

- it is possible to configure the class to provide _optional_before_ arguments with no _required_ arguments
- it is possible to set the same name of an argument to both _required_ and _optional_before_

I suggest that appropriate assertions and tests are added for such cases. We have a custom-built argument parser and we support extensions of both the library layer and CLI through subclassing. I think a fellow developer should be protected from such mistakes by the argument parser implementation.

well, what can i say, my view is different.

IMHO save() is broken because it causes a very high probability of loosing data, compared to any other saves the users normally do. to worsen things, the data lost is not the data being edited (the audio), and this is the worst scenario and something users won't understand or be lenient with. save() has to be fixed because it is broken and has not use case as it is. there is no point in introducing another API and leaving a broken one up. and expecting existing clients to change is not realistic.

replace() is not the solution, as it causes massive performance loss all the time, when most of the time it is not needed.

the probability that a 1KB structure gets partially written is abysmally low compared to the probability that a 100MB file gets partially written; maybe 100.000 times smaller. (i listen to dj sets mostly, so my individual files are typically over 100MB.) this means that around 100.000 corruption incidents of moved tracks would be observed before 1 tag-only corruption happens (if both operations are equally likely to be executed). but of course, before 100.000 corruptions happen your library would be eliminated from any successful app.

i guess 100.000 times safer than the current implementation can be called 'safe enough', given that you are not currently bombarded by thousands of corruption reports.

> Regarding the naming. I prefer replace() as it highlights the the file gets replaced as a whole, while when I read saveAs() I think of saving to another location

this is exactly what saveAs() does: saves a new file. i proposed that omitting the argument causes save as temp file and replacement of the original. ok, maybe this is not the best interface. so instead: saveAs() must be called with a file argument, and the file must not exist. saveAtomically(), saveViaCopy(), atomicSave(), saveSafely(), safeSave() are all possible names of "saveAs() to temp and then atomically replace". the docs should state that although atomicSave() is safer, save() should be is safe enough for all uses. (implementation of atomicSave() has a low priority IMHO because it will almost never be used. fixing save() has a high priority.)

replace() is a bad name, and the semantics are not at all clear. why would you call replace("xxx.mp3") to save a new xxx.mp3 file? and if you want to save to a new file, why would the provided API silently replace any existing file instead of complaining that it exists? and most importantly: if the file exists, would replace() replace it atomically? if not, how would you document that? if yes, then there is a 3rd filename involved that is not being specified!

my semantics:
save() is save in-place. reasonably safe and reasonably performant. (creates a temp copy only if a dangerous operation is needed.)
saveAs() is save to new file. no ugly replacement semantics: the method just returns an error if the file exists.
saveAtomically() is an ultra-safe, ultra slow implementation of save in-place. included for completeness and because it is easy to implement once you have saveAs().

> What amount of programs do atomic replace for your documents?

all of them i suppose. the problem is that some apps didn't do an fsync so data losses happened all the same when people changed file systems. you can read about it: https://lwn.net/Articles/322823/

the point is that data loss happens a lot. if your library is used that is; if nobody uses it, then yes, whatever you do here is just fine :). but the idea is that a library must be robust **just in case** it becomes successful. this is why IMHO save() has to be fixed.

i know that adding code to implement a decision to copy or not before starting the save process to the existing code base can be painful. but you don't need to do that. you can start saving in-place and only create a temp when you need to scroll big data. after the scroll, you immediately replace atomically, and then continue to save in-place. this is an easy change (only the scroll operation has to be modified) and yet it adds a lot of reliability.

this highlights that saveAs()/saveAtomically() are not necessarily needed to solve (and are not the solution to) save()'s issues. saveAs()/saveAtomically(), if desired, can be added at a later stage.

(of course, in a perfect world you would detect scroll before starting save() and use saveAs() instead.)

@wintersteiger Thanks for merging. The cause of the problem is a combination of two issues

* ``csc.exe`` behaves strangely (from my perspective). It seems that the argument given to ``/linkresource:`` needs to refer to a file in the same directory that will contain ``Microsoft.Z3.dll``. If I give ``/linkresource:`` an absolute path to ``libz3.dll`` (in a different directory than ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` will reside) then the build of ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` succeeds but when I try to build the example .NET application  and add a reference to ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` then Visual Studio complains that it can't find ``libz3.dll`` in the directory containing ``Microsoft.Z3.dll`` (i.e. despite giving an absolute path to the ``/linkresource`` flag that path is ignored and it only seems to use the file name).
* Multi-configuration CMake generators (i.e. Visual Studio and XCode). With these generators it currently doesn't seem possible to have custom rules (``add_custom_command()``) output files into the build directory of another target without very horrible hacks. For single configuration generators (e.g. Makefiles, NMake files, Ninja...) it is trivial to do this.

When experimenting I found for single configuration CMake generators I could use the ``/linkresource`` flag and have everything work correctly but the build would fail for multi-configuration CMake generators (i.e. Visual Studio). I could have just have ``/linkresource`` passed only for single configuration generators but I thought that would be annoyingly inconsistent. So I decided to not pass the ``/linkresource`` flag in any type of build.

We might be able to avoid these issues if the Z3 .NET bindings were simply installed on Windows (rather than trying to use them from the build directory) as they are under all other distributions. Is there a reason we don't install the .NET bindings or is this not best practice under Windows?

I have a first proto-type ready for you both to take a look at

You can find my fork at https://github.com/delcypher/z3/tree/travis_ci_proto_type

You can see some example builds at

https://travis-ci.org/delcypher/z3/builds/247161333

You can click on any of the configurations and see the build log.

Here is a rough tour of how it works

1. The [`.travis.yml`](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/.travis.yml) file in the root of the repository is a special file that TravisCI looks for that tells it how to test Z3.
2. Under the `env.matrix` key in the file you'll find a list. Each item in the list is setting various environment variables which controls the configuration of the build.
3. The `.travis.yml` contains the line `contrib/ci/scripts/travis_ci_entry_point.sh`. This points to the script that will run the build.
4. The [`contrib/ci/scripts/travis_ci_entry_point.sh`](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/contrib/ci/scripts/travis_ci_entry_point.sh) script determines if it is running on Travis's Linux infrastructure or OSX infrastructure  and calls the appropriate script. I've not implemented the OSX support yet.
5. The [`contrib/ci/scripts/travis_ci_linux_entry_point.sh`](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/contrib/ci/scripts/travis_ci_linux_entry_point.sh) script is essentially a wrapper around the `docker build` command. We use docker for the linux infrastructure because it gives us full control of the dependencies and linux distribution, and makes it easy to reproduce builds locally. The script consumes the environment variables that were set in `.travis.yml` and passes them to the `docker build` command if appropriate.
6. The build steps for docker are described in [`contrib/ci/Dockerfiles/z3_base_ubuntu_14.04.Dockerfile`](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/contrib/ci/Dockerfiles/z3_base_ubuntu_14.04.Dockerfile). This Dockerfile installs all the dependencies we need and then calls various scripts in `contrib/ci/scripts` to do parts of the build and test various parts of it.

Some additional notes

* There's no OSX support right now. The scripts are written in a reasonably portable manner so they should easily be re-purposed to build and test Z3.
* The Dockerfile isn't really ideal. I've coupled together building and installing the dependencies. These could be decoupled and the "install dependencies" stage cached to potentially give faster builds.
* The Dockerfile isn't ideal for local testing. Docker's internal cache gets invalidated unnecessarily because I was lazy about what files get copied from Z3's repo and when. This can be improved but at the expense of readability of the Dockerfile. As this is an early proto-type I decided to go for readability rather than performance.
* We could easily add Dockerfiles for other Linux distributions (e.g. Ubuntu 16.04 LTS, CentOS). I just haven't done this for now for simplicity.
* Building and testing a single configuration currently takes ~30 minutes. I don't know if that is considered acceptable.
* TravisCI has no windows support. AppVeyour does but this is a problem I'd rather let someone else handle.
* There's no deployment of build artefacts (e.g. uploading generated documentation and binaries somewhere). This is something TravisCI supports but for simplicity this is not implemented right now.
* When TravisCI is running you can actually follow the build as its running (i.e. the console output dynamically updates in your web browser) which is a super cool feature :)

The parts that need special attention from you both are

* [contrib/ci/scripts/test_z3_examples_cmake.sh](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/contrib/ci/scripts/test_z3_examples_cmake.sh) which is the script that builds and test Z3's API examples
* [z3_src/contrib/ci/scripts/test_z3_unit_tests_cmake.sh](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/contrib/ci/scripts/test_z3_unit_tests_cmake.sh) which is the script that builds Z3's unit tests (`z3-test`).
* [z3_src/contrib/ci/scripts/test_z3_system_tests.sh](https://github.com/delcypher/z3/blob/travis_ci_proto_type/contrib/ci/scripts/test_z3_system_tests.sh) which is the script that runs the tests in the `z3test` repo. This needs extra special attention because I've never used this repo before or its scripts so I don't know what I'm doing.

> It's still WIP, in particular I would like your opinion on Line 63, I'm not sure what's the best way to check if we need to use the training op or the inference op.

As stated above:
> The Theano testing Op is not useful for our implementation, since it would require the variances, not the inverse standard deviations.

So we'd never use the inference Op.

Your `if deterministic` currently checks whether we need the training Op at all. This should probably be `if update_averages or not use_averages`, since those are the cases where we'd either need the input statistics, or the input normalized by the input statistics. The `if update_averages` stays as before. In the end, you'll need to check `if use_averages` to decide whether to return the existing `normalized`, or compute it from `self.mean` and `self.inv_std`. The `deterministic` flag only gives defaults for `batch_norm_use_averages` and `batch_norm_update_averages` in case they're not given; it should not be checked for directly later on.

About `class BatchNormDNNLayer(Layer):`: You may want to derive it from the existing `BatchNormLayer`, so you don't need to replicate parameter initialization and such. Note that cuDNN does not allow to omit beta and gamma, though, if I remember correctly. Check whether the Op handles this, otherwise you'll have to create tensor constants to keep it happy.

> Moreover, from the first tests, it doesn't that the cudnn operation dramatically improves the speed as I expected from your previous benchmark.

Did you time the batch normalization separately or as part of a larger network?

If it is Ok that the full hierarchy of the file is being setup for the "auxiliary" files than the simplest way would be right now to just:

1) Create a separate NWB file for each stimulus
2) Create a link to the appropriate stimulus container when creating a main data file.

This is possible right now and should be an Ok workflow. I think the main question is whether we want to allow "partial" files, i.e., files that contain only, e.g., a stimulus container but not the parts specified by NWB file. From a high-level, there are two ways to go about this:

1) Make it a function of the HDF5IO backend, e.g., to allow write single NWBContainer's other than NWBFile 
2) Make it explicitly part of the schema by creating a schema for NWBAuxilitaryFile

To me this feels more like a backed-specific function rather than a schema issue, so I think (1) is be an Ok option. In fact, FORMIO only expects a container, i.e.,  (1) is basically possible right now, because you could, e.g., create a TimeSeries and hand it to HDF5IO for writing without ever creating an NWBFile for it. E.g., the following works right now:

```
from pynwb import TimeSeries, NWBHDF5IO
import numpy as np
# Create an example timeseries
test_ts = TimeSeries('test_timeseries', 'PyNWB tutorial', np.arange(200000).reshape((100,200,10)), 'SIunit', timestamps=np.arange(10))
# Write just the timeseries to file
io = NWBHDF5IO('basic_example.nwbaux', 'w')
io.write(test_ts)
io.close()
```

The result is an HDF5 file with the TimeSeries as the root, i.e., the file has the datasets ``/data`` and ``/timestamps`` as well as all the standard attributes of the TimeSeries (e.g., ``neurodata_type``, ``description``, ``help`` etc.) associated with the root "/" group. I'm not sure right now how the linking of that TimeSeries works but I don't have the time today to dig deeper into this issue.

Ok, this change is generally good! I've got lots of little notes, but they're almost all style and not substance, so we're in a good place.

The only thing that's bothering me is that I think we're breaking the contract of deque. Generally speaking for an indexable iterable `iterable[len(iterable)]` should return `IndexError`.

They *aren't* true of this deque. This means you actually can't use it everywhere you could use a deque, and anything that expects a deque will be royally confused. It's also weird that `table.appendleft((name, val))` works, but then `table[0]` does not return `(name, val)`.

I appreciate that we want to be able to do the handy-dandy `__getitem__` shorthand, but it might be better to just not allow that. If we stop allowing that, and instead have `get_by_index()` as a method, I think everything goes back to behaving just fine.

**Note** I've used the ternary operator for a condensed post here, feel free to expand it if you don't like it. Also I've used the assumption that the jobs are in a dictionary which is keyed on the jobid which I think is correct.

**Note also** The subtle difference from a normal indexable list in that calling `getitem` will in the case of positive integers get the corresponding entry from the dictionary i.e. `jobs[3]` will get the job with id 3 even if it's the first job in the jobs list. However with negative integers we return to the usual list behaviour of `jobs[-1]` getting the last element. I think that this is the most intuitive user expected response as they expect a mapping by jobid.

Right, BrowserID decentralizes identity, but not service discovery, so we still need something like WebFinger.

So WebFinger could announce:
* The URL of the server
* The API of the server (would be nice to keep it generic, so the same mechanism can be used for announcing Kinto-compatible as well as remoteStorage-compatible, CouchDB-compatible, *-compatible servers)
* Any available end-points for getting access through BrowserId/FxA
* Any available end-points for getting access through OAuth2 implicit grant flow

WebFinger links need a "rel" attribute to indicate what they mean. For remoteStorage discovery, we use this: https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-dejong-remotestorage-05#section-10 - might serve as a basis for something generic enough to also discover servers with `<storage_api> == "kinto-v1"`.

The remoteStorage spec currently used the "http://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6749#section-4.2" property for announcing the dialog URL for OAuth2 Implicit Grant, but if we want a "discover the user's storage server of any kind" mechanism, we can add a longer list of possible auth mechanisms.

My main concern is how to treat permissions. Since there is no support to sets on JSON Object notation. I've choose to treat them as dicts. Permission operations would look like:

- ``{"op": "add", "path": "/permissions/read/alice", "value": ""}``
- ``{"op": "add", "path": "/permissions/read/alice"}`` (I've also add the option to omit ``value`` on this case).
- ``{"op": "remove", "path": "/permissions/read/alice"}``

Another approach would be to treat permissions as lists, but the remove option would need to be specified as a numeric value, which I think is not consistent with the use case presented.

I've also wasn't able to remove the read dependency on ``__permissions__``, but permissions are only updated on ``SharableResource.process_record()``.

On line 436 of `backend-v4l2.cpp`, macros `major()` and `minor()` are used. It appears that starting from glibc-2.25 the macros `major` and `minor` are only available from `sys/sysmacros.h` include file:

> The inclusion of <sys/sysmacros.h> by <sys/types.h> is deprecated.
  This means that in a future release, the macros “major”, “minor”, and
  “makedev” will only be available from <sys/sysmacros.h>.
>
> These macros are not part of POSIX nor XSI, and their names frequently
  collide with user code; see for instance glibc bug 19239 and Red Hat
  bug 130601.  <stdlib.h> includes <sys/types.h> under _GNU_SOURCE, and
  C++ code presently cannot avoid being compiled under _GNU_SOURCE,
  exacerbating the problem.

Hence the simple solution is that `sys/sysmacros.h` should be included on all Linux distributions not just Android, which I did in this PR. Better solution would be to get rid of those macros altogether.

I think these are fair points and thanks for the detailed reply, @montoyjh! I agree, getting FunctionFeaturizer to mesh with BaseFeaturizer is a little trickier than the other ones, as it takes a dataframe as input rather than a material. That said, I still think we can make it work and work sanely without modifying the behavior of any methods inherited from BaseFeaturizer.

The choice of the word `fit` is somewhat misleading and is a result of us conforming to scikit-learn's BaseEstimator/Transformer API. It is better to think of the "fit" operation as "configure." The main point of the fit operation is to set any options of the featurizer that are not known when initializing the featurizer and are dependent on a training set. We separate "fit" from the featurization operations to prevent the nature of features changing between when a featurizer is used on multiple datasets (e.g., a training and test set). The number of features or how they are computed changing between training and test sets, of course, would be a huge issue. 

FunctionFeaturizer does not really need the `fit` operation. As shown in how [PolynomialFeatures](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/a24c8b46/sklearn/preprocessing/data.py#L1180) - a close analogy to your class - is implemented, `fit` only sets the number of inputs for checking purposes and computes the number of outputs for use when allocating the output array. While these are helpful, if you make the assumption that your user will always pass the same type of data to `transform`/`featurize` or need to optimize memory usage then `fit` can be eliminated.

I you agree with this logic, here is my proposal for changes FunctionFeaturizer:

- `fit` - No changes to base functionality. It could be made more robust by making `input_feature_names` optional by generating fake names (e.g., "x0", "x1") if none are provided. Additionally, it would be nice to have type checking so that if `X` is a DataFrame, we read the column labels from DataFrame.

- `featurize_dataframe` - Remove call to `fit`, so that `featurize_dataframe` will not change the state of the featurizer. Here, the reasoning is to provide some notion that the featurizer is configured on the training dataset and then will not change behavior on any future calls.

If I understand right, then the biggest change to using FunctionFeaturizer will be a need to call `fit` before `featurize_dataframe`. I think this is acceptable, as this is how scikit-learn's feature expansion tools (i.e., PolynomialFeatures) is used.

Specifying a relative path does not work either.

With the following config:
```
embeddings {
  tensor_name: "scope/first_tensor_name:0"
  metadata_path: "../not-logdir/first_dict"
}
```
Tensorboard shows the following error message:

> Error fetching metadata
> "gs://bucket/logdir/../not-logdir/first_dict" not found, or is not a file

Which can be expected, since os.path.join() can't handle relative paths.
I was looking into [`os.path.normpath()`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/os.path.html#os.path.normpath) to collapse the relative path, but unfortunately it also removes double slashes:

```python
>>> dict_path = os.path.join('gs://bucket/logdir', '../not-logdir/first_dict')
# 'gs://bucket/logdir/../not-logdir/first_dict'
>>> os.path.normpath(dict_path)
# 'gs:/bucket/not-logdir/first_dict'
```

Nice work @DiogoAndre! Just looking skimming the code there are a few things I can comment on.

First you are using the old test framework, to save time later and not have to recreate the mock data it would be better to use the new framework (the one used by napalm-eos and napalm-ios).

Secondly it can be a good idea to write the code to be compliant with both Python 2 and Python 3 at the same time, this also saves time if it's done from the start.

Just looking at the code my only real comment is around this line:
``` Python
ssl._create_default_https_context = ssl._create_unverified_context
```
First I think this requires Python 2.7.9. I'm not really sure about which version of Python 2.7 that Napalm targets. Granted 2.7.9 is quite old but a lot of distros still doesn't use that version. More importantly I don't think it's a good idea to ignore the certificate security. While I don't know how other Napalm drivers which use https handle certificates. It would be a good idea to keep that behaviour consistent, in terms of setting trusted certificates and such.

A thing to consider later on with the config operations is something like this which would be valid with the other Napalm drivers:
```
clear configure access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 remark --- Traffic from NET-SOHO-PATRICK to INTERNET
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 remark - ICMP/8, ECHO
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 extended permit icmp object-group NET-SOHO-PATRICK object-group INTERNET echo
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 remark - TCP/21, FTP
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 extended permit tcp object-group NET-SOHO-PATRICK object-group INTERNET eq ftp
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 remark - TCP/22, SSH
access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 extended permit tcp object-group NET-SOHO-PATRICK object-group INTERNET eq ssh

access-list ACL-WORK-V1 remark - TCP/443, HTTPS
access-list ACL-WORK-V1 extended permit tcp object-group NET-WORK object-group INTERNET eq https

crypto ikev2 policy 50
 encryption 3des
 integrity sha
 group 2
 prf sha
 lifetime seconds 86400
```

Where we have an access-list ACL-INSIDE-V5 which we want to ensure it looks exactly like shown above, while for ACL-WORK-V1 we only want to make sure that https is permitted but we don't care about what the rest of the access-list looks like. As an example we also want a specific ikev2 policy created. In order to make this idempotent the driver would first have to parse the input and figure out if the ACL-INSIDE-V5 looks like we want it to do below the clear configure line. For that part of the config do a replace operation in the API, for the ACL-WORK-V1 it would need an update/patch operation. It might be that something like Jason suggested will be needed to handle this in a simple way.

Thanks for the test code!  I investigated this some more.  It was a bit subtle.

The diamond case here actually works properly: It's the *non-diamond* case, i.e. single inheritance from a virtual base class, where we run into trouble.  But in the test case as written in the first commit, registering the diamond case actually fixed both cases!

The core issue is that we aren't detecting virtual inheritance as something special: we treat it as ordinary inheritance, which means when we upcast we just `reinterpret_cast` the derived pointer to its parent.  This isn't valid under virtual inheritance (though it happens to work under gcc/clang), so we need to add some detection here.

We *do* have a path for proper casting, but it's more complicated, and so we try to only invoke it when necessary: which, currently, means either the class is a base or some ancestor of a base in a registered multiple inheritance class; or you manually specify `py::multiple_inheritance{}` as an argument to the `py::class_<...>` constructor (which is designed for telling pybind that multiple inheritance is involved but not being exposed to Python).  We need to fix this so that it also triggers when specifying a single, virtual base class.

Now as for your test code, the original commit ended up masking the issue because the diamond inheritance registration marked the `Interface` base class as non-simple, which then made the non-multiple, virtual inheritance take the slower (but correct) casting path.

The second one by itself, however, already works fine: when the `py::class_<Diamond, B_Concrete, C_Concrete>(m, "Diamond")` is registered, it recursively marks known ancestors as "non-simple", avoiding the simple path.

I've amended the test code in the PR to duplicate the base `Interface` (to avoid the second registration accidentally fixing the first one), which let the first case trigger the failure again without having to disable the second test.  (I also moved some things around to make the style more consistent with the other pybind test code, but that's mainly cosmetic).

I've also merged a fix into the commit to detect the virtual base, so these tests should pass now.  (While I was at it, I squashed and rebased against `master`).

For current stable, a simple workaround is to add a `py::multiple_inheritance{}` option to the derived `class_` constructor, i.e.:

```C++
py::class_<Derived, VirtualBase>(m, "Derived", py::multiple_inheritance{})
```

Since this commit ends up a bit big (i.e. it required adding a new header, to avoid an unconditional gcc warning in the implementation of the virtual base detection), *and* because the workaround above is pretty easy, *and* because this is a fairly rare issue (virtual inheritance is not useful without also having multiple inheritance lurking), I'm leaning against pushing this to to 2.2.x branch.

The current ACME spec says `tls-sni-01` should be done with multiple hash iterations (the RFC draft suggests 25), given by the "`n`" parameter. Yes, pass the validator into the appropriate functions. It's important to let oauthlib do the talking to your validator, because it does a bunch of special handling to make sure a lot of stuff with oauth, especially to do with timing, is done carefully.

Also, `nonce_length` can be set as a class property, rather than adding it to the class after instantiation. That's the way I would do it, at least, and then you can just create an instance of the validator inside your call to `is_valid_request` like `tool_provider.is_valid_request(LTIValidator())`.

So the validator you gave as an example could look more like this:

```python
class LTIValidator(RequestValidator):
    nonce_length = (5, 50)

    # No need to override `__init__` if you're not changing it.

    def validate_timestamp_and_nonce(self, client_key, timestamp, nonce,
                                     request, request_token=None, access_token=None):
        # My implementation of validator
        pass
```

> Why not? Why closing the http request is not good enough?

I actually am not sure. For the Webui we have to ask @andrevmatos 

> I don't think so, timeouts don't compose well.

I really don't see this as a nice solution usability wise. Compare these 2 user scenarios.

### Scenario 1

Alice starts a transfer via curl. She gets no ACK from BoB after `X` seconds and the timeout kicks in, stopping the transfer attempt, finishing the http request and informing her that the transfer timed out and that BoB is probably offline.

### Scenario 2

Alice starts a transfer via curl. She gets no ACK from BoB after `X` seconds. She keeps waiting. At some point she will start wondering what the heck is wrong, will have to read the docs and find out that this is expected behaviour if BoB is offline and that she should take action to close the http request manually.
### Clarification

Scenario 1 is a thousand times better for our users.

What do you mean by "compose well".

When we have the direct transfers as parts of a mediated transfer and we have more than one timeouts to consider? If so we can specify the timeout only for simple direct transfers.

Actually, we can use the `transferred_amount` to order the messages (it's also a monotonically increasing value), the nonce can be moved out of the envelope into a transport detail of Raiden.
@palango yes :)

About the URL+JSON, definitely my proposal was wrong, completely agree that we need to change to plural and remove the `transfer_amount` from the URL in favor of JSON. One suggestion, perhaps we should use the PUT verb instead of POST with the identifier of the transfer in the URL?

About the HTTP ERROR codes:

[408 - REQUEST TIMEOUT](https://httpstatuses.com/408): I had gave this one a bit of thought before, the main problem is that 4xx are _client_ errors, and 408 is used when the _client_ didn't send the data to the server in due time, so the server closes the connection because it's not willing to wait anymore. That's not really what we want, we want to inform the client that a downstream timeout happened. We could overload this code for our purposes, but I'm not 100% sure if that is a good idea. (I have looked at most of the HTTP ERROR codes and I didn't find any that matches our needs exactly. Actually for this case a 5xx 
error is probably better).

[409 - CONFLICT](https://httpstatuses.com/409): Sounds good to use this one.

@Dominik-Vogel perhaps it would be useful to explain my reasoning behind using add_submodule as opposed to extending add_parameter. Given that multi-threading has been taken out of qcodes for now, the only reason that add_parameter/add_function even needs to be used (as opposed to something like `self.param = Parameter("bla", ...)`) was to ensure snapshotting works. However the way that objects are accessed from the `parameters` dictionary meant that it wasn't really logical to store more general objects in this dictionary. The submodules dictionary was defined to store **ANY** instance of a `Metadatable` object. 

It's used in a couple of places for more than just channels, see the yokogawa driver linked above for a more general usage example. Or, in the [Lakeshore.Model_336](https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument_drivers/Lakeshore/Model_336.py) driver, add_submodule is used to allow access to the temperature channels either using the channel list OR using the channel letters, i.e. the following two methods of accessing the channel are equivalent:
```py3
temp = Model_336("temp", "GPIB_ADDRESS")
temp.channels[0].temperature.get()
temp.A.temperature.get()
```

@AdriaanRol Actually, rather interestingly, there seems to be a conflict in the way we both access parameters, which may decide how we move forward. We normally pull parameters out of Instruments and call get/set on these. You seem to call get/set on the instrument with a parameter name as an argument? The ChannelList/InstrumentChannel divide was to support the former method of access, and we can write down a mapping between all your examples, and how I would access them currently:

| Parameter Based Access | Instrument Based Access |
| --- | --- |
| `mySMU.source.range.set(5)` | `mySMU.set('source.range', 5)` |
| `VSM.in[0].out[0].att.get()` | `VSM.get('in1.out1.att)` |
| `VSM.in.out.switch.set('OFF')` | `VSM.set('in*.out*.switch, 'OFF')` |
| `VSM.in[0].out[0:2].set([0, 2])` | `VSM.set('in1.out*.att', [0, 2])` |
| `VSM.in[0].out[2:4].set([0, 2])` | `?` |

To answer @AdriaanRol's question then:
- `InstrumentChannel` is an **instrument-like** object that points all `ask/write` requests to the parent instrument.
 - `ChannelList` is a **list-like** object that implements slicing logic for channel lists (i.e. makes `dac.channels[0:20]` possible)
 - `MultiChannelInstrumentParamter` is a **parameter-like** object that contains multiple instrument channels (i.e. the result of `dac.channels[0:20].voltage` is a `MultiChannelInstrumentParamter` pointing to the voltage parameter of the first 20 dac channels. It is like a `CombinedParameter` that points to the first 20 channels of the dac.

My goal with this was to get rid of strings as a method of getting access to channels, and to allow list-like access to channels. In addition, to allow a more structured definition of large instrument drivers by breaking out sections as logical. Code generated channel lists are still very much possible (see [Decadac](https://github.com/QCoDeS/Qcodes/blob/master/qcodes/instrument_drivers/Harvard/Decadac.py) Driver), although this may require more work for JSON code generation.

Hi, I wonder do you have any plan to fix this issue. It seems that currently Ray cannot be used to train and tune models with cheap spot machines, which is really a pity. 

I was looking at the source code, but I am not sure what is the best way to fix it. As an initial step, I wrote a wrapper on `ray.wait` to call `reconstruct_objects` every 2 seconds if the required objects are not ready. This code can pass existing tests and is able to reconstruct missing objects when the  worker node is dead. 

Any comments would be greatly appreciated


```py


def wait(object_ids, num_returns=1, timeout=None, worker=global_worker):
    worker.check_connected()

    if worker.mode == LOCAL_MODE:
        return object_ids[:num_returns], object_ids[num_returns:]

    TIMEOUT_PER_ITERATION = 2000
    remain_time = timeout or int(2 ** 30)
    fetch_request_size = (ray._config.worker_fetch_request_size())
    ready_ids_all = []
    remaining_ids = object_ids

    while remain_time > 0 and len(ready_ids_all) < num_returns and remaining_ids:
        curr_time = time.time()
        wait_time = min(TIMEOUT_PER_ITERATION, remain_time)
        ready_ids, remaining_ids = _wait(remaining_ids, num_returns, wait_time, worker)
        ready_ids_all.extend(ready_ids)

        for i in range(0, len(remaining_ids), fetch_request_size):
            if worker.use_raylet:
                worker.local_scheduler_client.reconstruct_objects(
                    remaining_ids[i: i + fetch_request_size], False)
            else:
                for unready_id in remaining_ids[i: i + fetch_request_size]:
                    worker.local_scheduler_client.reconstruct_objects(
                        [unready_id], False)

        remain_time -= (time.time() - curr_time) * 1000

    return ready_ids_all, remaining_ids

# just renamed wait to _wait
def _wait(object_ids, num_returns=1, timeout=None, worker=global_worker):
    """Return a list of IDs that are ready and a list of IDs that are not.

    If timeout is set, the function returns either when the requested number of
    IDs are ready or when the timeout is reached, whichever occurs first. If it
    is not set, the function simply waits until that number of objects is ready
    and returns that exact number of object IDs.

    This method returns two lists. The first list consists of object IDs that
    correspond to objects that are available in the object store. The second
    list corresponds to the rest of the object IDs (which may or may not be
    ready).

    Ordering of the input list of object IDs is preserved. That is, if A
    precedes B in the input list, and both are in the ready list, then A will
    precede B in the ready list. This also holds true if A and B are both in
    the remaining list.

    Args:
        object_ids (List[ObjectID]): List of object IDs for objects that may or
            may not be ready. Note that these IDs must be unique.
        num_returns (int): The number of object IDs that should be returned.
        timeout (int): The maximum amount of time in milliseconds to wait
            before returning.

    Returns:
        A list of object IDs that are ready and a list of the remaining object
        IDs.
    """

    if isinstance(object_ids, ray.ObjectID):
        raise TypeError(
            "wait() expected a list of ObjectID, got a single ObjectID")

    if not isinstance(object_ids, list):
        raise TypeError("wait() expected a list of ObjectID, got {}".format(
            type(object_ids)))

    if worker.mode != LOCAL_MODE:
        for object_id in object_ids:
            if not isinstance(object_id, ray.ObjectID):
                raise TypeError("wait() expected a list of ObjectID, "
                                "got list containing {}".format(
                    type(object_id)))

    worker.check_connected()
    with profiling.profile("ray.wait", worker=worker):
        # When Ray is run in LOCAL_MODE, all functions are run immediately,
        # so all objects in object_id are ready.
        if worker.mode == LOCAL_MODE:
            return object_ids[:num_returns], object_ids[num_returns:]

        # TODO(rkn): This is a temporary workaround for
        # https://github.com/ray-project/ray/issues/997. However, it should be
        # fixed in Arrow instead of here.
        if len(object_ids) == 0:
            return [], []

        if len(object_ids) != len(set(object_ids)):
            raise Exception("Wait requires a list of unique object IDs.")
        if num_returns <= 0:
            raise Exception(
                "Invalid number of objects to return %d." % num_returns)
        if num_returns > len(object_ids):
            raise Exception("num_returns cannot be greater than the number "
                            "of objects provided to ray.wait.")
        timeout = timeout if timeout is not None else 2 ** 30
        if worker.use_raylet:
            ready_ids, remaining_ids = worker.local_scheduler_client.wait(
                object_ids, num_returns, timeout, False)
        else:
            object_id_strs = [
                plasma.ObjectID(object_id.id()) for object_id in object_ids
            ]
            ready_ids, remaining_ids = worker.plasma_client.wait(
                object_id_strs, timeout, num_returns)
            ready_ids = [
                ray.ObjectID(object_id.binary()) for object_id in ready_ids
            ]
            remaining_ids = [
                ray.ObjectID(object_id.binary()) for object_id in remaining_ids
            ]
        return ready_ids, remaining_ids


```
